questions.stem: In the process shown at this replication fork, what is the primary role of the enzyme that acts on the parental duplex ahead of the polymerases?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It relieves supercoiling by transiently nicking the backbone

choices.label: B
choices.text: It synthesizes short RNA primers to initiate synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It unwinds and separates the two parental strands to expose single-stranded templates

choices.label: D
choices.text: It joins Okazaki fragments by forming phosphodiester bonds

choices.label: E
choices.text: It adds free nucleotides to the growing 3' end of the new strand

explanation.summary: The teardrop-shaped enzyme at the fork is helicase, which breaks hydrogen bonds to unwind parental DNA; the polymerases then extend new strands using the exposed templates and free nucleotides.
explanation.whyCorrect: The teardrop-shaped enzyme at the fork is helicase, which breaks hydrogen bonds to unwind parental DNA; the polymerases then extend new strands using the exposed templates and free nucleotides.

questions.stem: Why is the strand indicated at the lower right synthesized discontinuously as short segments requiring repeated RNA primers, while the upper strand is made continuously?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lagging-strand template is chemically different and resists polymerization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helicase moves too slowly for one strand to be copied at once

choices.label: C
choices.text: Topoisomerase actively cuts one strand into fragments during unwinding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Single-stranded binding proteins block continuous synthesis on one strand only

choices.label: E
choices.text: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides 5' to 3', so the strand templated in the direction away from the fork must be built in fragments

explanation.summary: Because polymerase extends only in the 5'-to-3' direction, the strand oriented opposite to fork movement is made as Okazaki fragments, each begun by an RNA primer, unlike the continuously synthesized leading strand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because polymerase extends only in the 5'-to-3' direction, the strand oriented opposite to fork movement is made as Okazaki fragments, each begun by an RNA primer, unlike the continuously synthesized leading strand.

questions.stem: What functional advantage does the ring-shaped structure encircling the DNA provide to the polymerase during replication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It tethers the polymerase to the template, greatly increasing processivity

choices.label: B
choices.text: It recruits primase to lay down new primers

choices.label: C
choices.text: It supplies the energy for phosphodiester bond formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It unwinds the double helix ahead of the polymerase

choices.label: E
choices.text: It proofreads and excises mismatched bases

explanation.summary: The sliding clamp is a ring that keeps polymerase attached to the DNA so it can add many nucleotides without dissociating, boosting the speed and processivity of replication.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sliding clamp is a ring that keeps polymerase attached to the DNA so it can add many nucleotides without dissociating, boosting the speed and processivity of replication.

questions.stem: The sequence of steps depicted resolves what problem left on the lagging strand after fragment synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relief of positive supercoils generated ahead of the fork

choices.label: B
choices.text: Removal of RNA primers and sealing of the nicks to produce a continuous DNA strand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Separation of the two daughter chromosomes at the centromere

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addition of a 5' methylguanosine cap to the transcript

choices.label: E
choices.text: Correction of a base mispaired during proofreading

explanation.summary: Strand-displacement synthesis pushes the RNA primer into a flap that is cleaved, then ligase seals the remaining nick, converting Okazaki fragments into an unbroken lagging strand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Strand-displacement synthesis pushes the RNA primer into a flap that is cleaved, then ligase seals the remaining nick, converting Okazaki fragments into an unbroken lagging strand.

questions.stem: The multi-step activity shown ensures which outcome during DNA synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Unwinding of the helix to expose the template

choices.label: B
choices.text: Attachment of the polymerase to the sliding clamp

choices.label: C
choices.text: High replication fidelity by excising a misincorporated base before continuing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Faster elongation by skipping damaged template regions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Generation of RNA primers for new fragments

explanation.summary: When an incorrect base is added, the polymerase's 3'-to-5' exonuclease activity backs up, removes the mispaired nucleotide, and then resumes synthesis with the correct base, ensuring proofreading fidelity.
explanation.whyCorrect: When an incorrect base is added, the polymerase's 3'-to-5' exonuclease activity backs up, removes the mispaired nucleotide, and then resumes synthesis with the correct base, ensuring proofreading fidelity.

questions.stem: The figure contrasts original and new strands in each daughter duplex. Which model of DNA replication does this illustrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conservative replication

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dispersive replication

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rolling-circle replication

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bidirectional theta replication

choices.label: E
choices.text: Semiconservative replication

explanation.summary: Each daughter helix retains one parental (original template) strand paired with one newly synthesized strand, which is the defining feature of semiconservative replication.
explanation.whyCorrect: Each daughter helix retains one parental (original template) strand paired with one newly synthesized strand, which is the defining feature of semiconservative replication.

questions.stem: At the highlighted point on the duplex, synthesis proceeds outward in both directions as shown below. What does this point represent and how does replication proceed from it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An origin of replication from which two forks move bidirectionally

choices.label: B
choices.text: A telomere that is extended in a single direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: A recombination hotspot where strands cross over

choices.label: D
choices.text: A promoter where transcription initiates unidirectionally

choices.label: E
choices.text: A centromere where sister chromatids are joined

explanation.summary: Replication begins at an origin and proceeds bidirectionally, forming two forks that travel in opposite directions, as shown by the diverging leading/lagging strand arrows.
explanation.whyCorrect: Replication begins at an origin and proceeds bidirectionally, forming two forks that travel in opposite directions, as shown by the diverging leading/lagging strand arrows.

questions.stem: Panel A schematizes the structures also seen by electron microscopy in panel B (red arrows). What advantage do these multiple structures give a large eukaryotic chromosome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They prevent recombination between sister chromatids

choices.label: B
choices.text: They mark sites where transcription is silenced

choices.label: C
choices.text: They protect chromosome ends from shortening

choices.label: D
choices.text: They ensure replication occurs in only one direction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Multiple origins allow the whole chromosome to be replicated quickly in parallel

explanation.summary: Numerous replication bubbles, each expanding from its own origin with two forks, let long eukaryotic DNA be copied simultaneously at many sites, so replication finishes in a reasonable time.
explanation.whyCorrect: Numerous replication bubbles, each expanding from its own origin with two forks, let long eukaryotic DNA be copied simultaneously at many sites, so replication finishes in a reasonable time.

questions.stem: After the enzyme at the branch point unwinds the parent duplex, what is the role of the beads coating the separated single strands?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They coat and stabilize single-stranded DNA, preventing it from reannealing

choices.label: B
choices.text: They synthesize the complementary daughter strand

choices.label: C
choices.text: They cut the backbone to relieve torsional strain

choices.label: D
choices.text: They add RNA primers to begin synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: They ligate adjacent fragments together

explanation.summary: Single-stranded DNA-binding proteins (SSB) bind the exposed strands produced by helicase, keeping them apart and protected until polymerase can copy them.
explanation.whyCorrect: Single-stranded DNA-binding proteins (SSB) bind the exposed strands produced by helicase, keeping them apart and protected until polymerase can copy them.

questions.stem: The numbered sequence depicts assembly of a complex on mRNA involving the 40S subunit, an initiator tRNA carrying methionine, and several eIFs before the 60S subunit joins. Which molecular event is required immediately before the large subunit can join in this process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cleavage of the poly-A tail from the mRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scanning of the mRNA and recognition of the AUG start codon

choices.label: C
choices.text: Binding of a release factor to a stop codon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removal of the 5' cap by a decapping enzyme

choices.label: E
choices.text: Peptidyl transferase formation of the first peptide bond

explanation.summary: This is eukaryotic translation initiation. The 43S pre-initiation complex scans the mRNA until the initiator Met-tRNA anticodon pairs with the AUG start codon; only after start-codon recognition do initiation factors dissociate and the 60S subunit join to form the 80S ribosome.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is eukaryotic translation initiation. The 43S pre-initiation complex scans the mRNA until the initiator Met-tRNA anticodon pairs with the AUG start codon; only after start-codon recognition do initiation factors dissociate and the 60S subunit join to form the 80S ribosome.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a distant DNA element bound by activator proteins looping around to contact a group of mediator proteins and general transcription factors assembled near the gene's promoter. What does this looping architecture accomplish?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It excises the promoter region from the chromosome

choices.label: B
choices.text: It replicates the DNA ahead of the polymerase

choices.label: C
choices.text: It lets a distant enhancer boost transcription initiation at the promoter

choices.label: D
choices.text: It exports the assembled complex out of the nucleus

choices.label: E
choices.text: It permanently silences the gene by DNA methylation

explanation.summary: Enhancers can act over long distances. DNA bending brings enhancer-bound activators into contact with the mediator complex and general transcription factors at the promoter, stabilizing RNA polymerase II recruitment and increasing the rate of transcription initiation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enhancers can act over long distances. DNA bending brings enhancer-bound activators into contact with the mediator complex and general transcription factors at the promoter, stabilizing RNA polymerase II recruitment and increasing the rate of transcription initiation.

questions.stem: The figure shows an enzyme sitting on double-stranded DNA that is locally unwound into a bubble, with a coding strand indicated. What process is this enzyme carrying out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reverse transcription of RNA into DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repair of a thymine dimer

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA replication at the origin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Translation of mRNA into protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transcription (synthesis of RNA from a DNA template)

explanation.summary: RNA polymerase unwinds a short region of DNA to form a transcription bubble and synthesizes RNA using the template strand; the coding (sense) strand has the same sequence as the RNA (with U for T). This is transcription.
explanation.whyCorrect: RNA polymerase unwinds a short region of DNA to form a transcription bubble and synthesizes RNA using the template strand; the coding (sense) strand has the same sequence as the RNA (with U for T). This is transcription.

questions.stem: Using the sentence 'THE FAT CAT ATE THE RAT,' the figure contrasts single-base insertion and deletion with a 3-base deletion. Why does the 3-base deletion at the bottom NOT scramble the downstream 'words'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Larger deletions are repaired before translation begins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deletions never affect the reading frame, only insertions do

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three deleted bases are always replaced by a stop codon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing a multiple of three bases preserves the downstream reading frame

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ribosome automatically corrects any deletion of bases

explanation.summary: The genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets. Inserting or deleting a number of bases not divisible by three shifts the reading frame, garbling all downstream codons (a frameshift). Deleting exactly three bases removes one codon but keeps the rest of the frame intact.
explanation.whyCorrect: The genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets. Inserting or deleting a number of bases not divisible by three shifts the reading frame, garbling all downstream codons (a frameshift). Deleting exactly three bases removes one codon but keeps the rest of the frame intact.

questions.stem: The figure shows the same string of numbers read in six different offset registers (+1, +2, +3, -1, -2, -3), with only one highlighted. What genetic concept is being illustrated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The six classes of enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: The six possible reading frames of a nucleotide sequence

choices.label: C
choices.text: The six energy levels of an electron shell

choices.label: D
choices.text: The six stages of the cell cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: The six histone proteins of the nucleosome

explanation.summary: A double-stranded sequence can be translated in three reading frames on each strand (three forward, three reverse), giving six total. Shifting the start point by one base changes which triplets are grouped, illustrating why the correct frame is essential for meaningful translation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A double-stranded sequence can be translated in three reading frames on each strand (three forward, three reverse), giving six total. Shifting the start point by one base changes which triplets are grouped, illustrating why the correct frame is essential for meaningful translation.

questions.stem: The three rows show the identical run of RNA bases grouped into triplets starting at different positions, each yielding a completely different peptide. What principle does this demonstrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wobble pairing changes the amino acid at every codon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Introns must be removed before any codon can be read

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reading frame determines which amino acids a sequence encodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: RNA can be translated in both directions simultaneously

choices.label: E
choices.text: Each amino acid is specified by a single base

explanation.summary: Because codons are read as non-overlapping triplets, the point at which grouping begins (the reading frame) dictates the entire peptide sequence. The same bases read in a different frame specify an entirely different set of amino acids.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because codons are read as non-overlapping triplets, the point at which grouping begins (the reading frame) dictates the entire peptide sequence. The same bases read in a different frame specify an entirely different set of amino acids.

questions.stem: In the leftmost column an AAG->AAA DNA change still yields the amino acid Lys. Why does this base substitution have no effect on the protein product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: tRNA cannot recognize a mutated codon and skips it

choices.label: B
choices.text: The genetic code is degenerate, so the new codon is synonymous and encodes the same amino acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: The substitution created a stop codon that was ignored

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mutation was repaired before translation occurred

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ribosome inserts the original amino acid by default

explanation.summary: Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (degeneracy of the code), often through third-position wobble. A substitution that still codes for the original residue is a silent (synonymous) mutation and leaves the protein sequence unchanged, unlike the nonsense and missense changes in the other columns.
explanation.whyCorrect: Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (degeneracy of the code), often through third-position wobble. A substitution that still codes for the original residue is a silent (synonymous) mutation and leaves the protein sequence unchanged, unlike the nonsense and missense changes in the other columns.

questions.stem: This chart groups the 20 standard amino acids into three boxes. What chemical property of the amino acid is used to assign each one to its group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The identity of the alpha-carboxyl group

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether it is a D- or L-stereoisomer

choices.label: C
choices.text: The molecular weight of the whole amino acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: The chemistry (polarity/charge) of the R-group side chain

choices.label: E
choices.text: The number of peptide bonds it can form

explanation.summary: Amino acids share a common backbone (amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon); they are classified by their distinguishing side chain (R-group) into nonpolar, polar-uncharged, and electrically charged (acidic/basic) categories.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amino acids share a common backbone (amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon); they are classified by their distinguishing side chain (R-group) into nonpolar, polar-uncharged, and electrically charged (acidic/basic) categories.

questions.stem: This figure shows four hierarchical organizational levels of a protein. Which single feature ultimately dictates all of the higher levels of folding shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The linear amino acid sequence

choices.label: B
choices.text: The presence of a lipid bilayer

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of subunits present

choices.label: D
choices.text: The concentration of free ribosomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ambient temperature of the cytosol

explanation.summary: The primary sequence (order of amino acids) encodes the information that determines how the chain folds into secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure; Anfinsen's principle shows sequence dictates native conformation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The primary sequence (order of amino acids) encodes the information that determines how the chain folds into secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure; Anfinsen's principle shows sequence dictates native conformation.

questions.stem: This pathway shows homocysteine being converted onward by a vitamin B6-dependent enzyme. A deficiency of the boxed enzyme causes which inherited disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Homocystinuria

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maple syrup urine disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cystinuria

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alkaptonuria

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phenylketonuria

explanation.summary: Cystathionine beta-synthase (vitamin B6 dependent) converts homocysteine to cystathionine. Its deficiency causes homocystinuria, with elevated homocysteine, marfanoid habitus, lens dislocation, and thrombosis risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cystathionine beta-synthase (vitamin B6 dependent) converts homocysteine to cystathionine. Its deficiency causes homocystinuria, with elevated homocysteine, marfanoid habitus, lens dislocation, and thrombosis risk.

questions.stem: This diagram shows how NADPH regenerates reduced glutathione to protect red cells from oxidative injury. Which metabolic pathway supplies the NADPH that drives this cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The citric acid (Krebs) cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway)

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pentose phosphate (HMP shunt) pathway

explanation.summary: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase is the rate-limiting enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway, which generates the NADPH needed to keep glutathione reduced; loss of this NADPH source leaves RBCs vulnerable to oxidative hemolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase is the rate-limiting enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway, which generates the NADPH needed to keep glutathione reduced; loss of this NADPH source leaves RBCs vulnerable to oxidative hemolysis.

questions.stem: This reaction shows the labeled enzyme converting one amino acid to another using BH4 and O2. Deficiency of this enzyme (or of its BH4 cofactor) causes which inherited disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phenylketonuria (PKU)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Homocystinuria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alkaptonuria

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maple syrup urine disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Albinism

explanation.summary: Phenylalanine hydroxylase converts phenylalanine to tyrosine using tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4). Its deficiency causes phenylketonuria, with phenylalanine accumulation, intellectual disability, and a musty odor if untreated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylalanine hydroxylase converts phenylalanine to tyrosine using tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4). Its deficiency causes phenylketonuria, with phenylalanine accumulation, intellectual disability, and a musty odor if untreated.

questions.stem: This enzyme-kinetics curve plots reaction velocity against substrate concentration. If one enzyme has a lower Km than another for the same substrate, what does that indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the enzyme is irreversibly inhibited

choices.label: B
choices.text: That the reaction is zero-order at all substrate levels

choices.label: C
choices.text: A higher maximum velocity (Vmax)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher affinity of the enzyme for its substrate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lower affinity for the substrate

explanation.summary: Km equals the substrate concentration at half Vmax. A lower Km means less substrate is needed to reach half-maximal velocity, reflecting greater enzyme-substrate affinity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Km equals the substrate concentration at half Vmax. A lower Km means less substrate is needed to reach half-maximal velocity, reflecting greater enzyme-substrate affinity.

questions.stem: The labeled enzyme catalyzes the interconversion shown between CO2/water and carbonic acid. This reaction is the central step of which physiologic function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CO2 transport and bicarbonate acid-base buffering

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium reabsorption in the loop of Henle

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP synthesis by oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ammonia detoxification in the urea cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oxygen loading onto hemoglobin

explanation.summary: Carbonic anhydrase rapidly interconverts CO2 and water with carbonic acid, which dissociates to bicarbonate and H+. This underlies CO2 transport in blood and the bicarbonate buffering system regulating acid-base balance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carbonic anhydrase rapidly interconverts CO2 and water with carbonic acid, which dissociates to bicarbonate and H+. This underlies CO2 transport in blood and the bicarbonate buffering system regulating acid-base balance.

questions.stem: This double-reciprocal plot of enzyme kinetics data is used to graphically determine Km and Vmax from the slope and intercepts. What is this type of plot called?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hill plot

choices.label: B
choices.text: Michaelis-Menten saturation curve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lineweaver-Burk plot

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scatchard plot

choices.label: E
choices.text: Eadie-Hofstee plot

explanation.summary: Plotting 1/vi against 1/[S] linearizes the Michaelis-Menten equation. The x-intercept gives -1/Km, the y-intercept gives 1/Vmax, and the slope is Km/Vmax. This is the classic Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot.
explanation.whyCorrect: Plotting 1/vi against 1/[S] linearizes the Michaelis-Menten equation. The x-intercept gives -1/Km, the y-intercept gives 1/Vmax, and the slope is Km/Vmax. This is the classic Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot.

questions.stem: This ATP-dependent reaction converts pyruvate and CO2 into oxaloacetate. Which enzyme catalyzes it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pyruvate dehydrogenase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyruvate carboxylase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pyruvate kinase

choices.label: E
choices.text: PEP carboxykinase

explanation.summary: Pyruvate carboxylase (a biotin-dependent, ATP-requiring enzyme) carboxylates pyruvate to oxaloacetate, an anaplerotic and gluconeogenic step. It is activated by acetyl-CoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pyruvate carboxylase (a biotin-dependent, ATP-requiring enzyme) carboxylates pyruvate to oxaloacetate, an anaplerotic and gluconeogenic step. It is activated by acetyl-CoA.

questions.stem: This table lists reaction rates for two isozymes across increasing substrate concentrations. Which conclusion is best supported by the data?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isozyme B has a lower Km (higher substrate affinity) than isozyme A

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two isozymes have identical Km values

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isozyme B has a lower substrate affinity than isozyme A

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isozyme A has a much higher Vmax than isozyme B

choices.label: E
choices.text: Isozyme A reaches half-maximal velocity at a lower [S] than isozyme B

explanation.summary: Both isozymes approach a Vmax near 200 umol/L/s. Isozyme B reaches half-maximal rate (~100) at about 5 mM, whereas isozyme A reaches it at about 10 mM. A lower [S] at half-Vmax means a lower Km and thus higher substrate affinity for isozyme B.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both isozymes approach a Vmax near 200 umol/L/s. Isozyme B reaches half-maximal rate (~100) at about 5 mM, whereas isozyme A reaches it at about 10 mM. A lower [S] at half-Vmax means a lower Km and thus higher substrate affinity for isozyme B.

questions.stem: These energy diagrams compare the same reaction run uncatalyzed (a) versus catalyzed (b). What does the catalyst accomplish, as illustrated by the difference between the curves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increases the overall free-energy change (delta G) of the reaction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shifts the equilibrium position toward products

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lowers the activation energy (Ea)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raises the free energy of the reactants

choices.label: E
choices.text: Makes the reaction more exergonic by lowering the free energy of the products

explanation.summary: A catalyst provides an alternate pathway with a smaller energy barrier, so the peak (Ea) is lower in panel (b). It does not change the energies of reactants or products, delta G, or the equilibrium position; it only speeds attainment of equilibrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: A catalyst provides an alternate pathway with a smaller energy barrier, so the peak (Ea) is lower in panel (b). It does not change the energies of reactants or products, delta G, or the equilibrium position; it only speeds attainment of equilibrium.

questions.stem: This sequence depicts an enzyme binding substrate (ES) and carrying the reaction over the free-energy peak (T superscript double-dagger) to product. How does the enzyme increase the reaction rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By supplying energy that shifts the equilibrium toward product

choices.label: B
choices.text: By permanently altering the substrate's covalent bonds before catalysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: By increasing the free energy of the product

choices.label: D
choices.text: By raising the ground-state energy of the substrate above the transition state

choices.label: E
choices.text: By stabilizing the transition state, which lowers its free energy

explanation.summary: Enzymes bind and stabilize the high-energy transition state more tightly than the substrate, lowering the activation energy peak and accelerating the reaction. They do not change the equilibrium or the net free-energy change.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enzymes bind and stabilize the high-energy transition state more tightly than the substrate, lowering the activation energy peak and accelerating the reaction. They do not change the equilibrium or the net free-energy change.

questions.stem: In this reaction glucose is converted to glucose-6-phosphate at the expense of ATP. What is the primary metabolic significance of this phosphorylation step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It fully oxidizes glucose to pyruvate

choices.label: B
choices.text: It traps glucose inside the cell because the charged phosphate prevents it from crossing the membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: It releases free glucose into the bloodstream

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly generates a net gain of ATP

choices.label: E
choices.text: It converts glucose into fructose

explanation.summary: Adding a negatively charged phosphate to glucose produces glucose-6-phosphate, which cannot diffuse back out through the membrane. This commits glucose to intracellular metabolism (glycolysis, glycogenesis, or the pentose phosphate pathway).
explanation.whyCorrect: Adding a negatively charged phosphate to glucose produces glucose-6-phosphate, which cannot diffuse back out through the membrane. This commits glucose to intracellular metabolism (glycolysis, glycogenesis, or the pentose phosphate pathway).

questions.stem: This reaction uses inorganic phosphate (Pi) to cleave a terminal glucose unit from glycogen, yielding glucose-1-phosphate. It is the rate-limiting step of which process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycogenolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gluconeogenesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lipolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycogenesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Glycolysis

explanation.summary: Glycogen phosphorylase catalyzes phosphorolysis of the alpha-1,4 bonds of glycogen, releasing glucose-1-phosphate. This is the rate-limiting, regulated step of glycogen breakdown (glycogenolysis).
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycogen phosphorylase catalyzes phosphorolysis of the alpha-1,4 bonds of glycogen, releasing glucose-1-phosphate. This is the rate-limiting, regulated step of glycogen breakdown (glycogenolysis).

questions.stem: In this pathway only the final step liberates free glucose. Which tissue can therefore use this pathway to raise blood glucose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Brain neurons

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adipose tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liver (it expresses glucose-6-phosphatase)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Skeletal muscle

explanation.summary: Only the liver (and kidney) express glucose-6-phosphatase, which dephosphorylates glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose for export. Skeletal muscle lacks this enzyme, so muscle glycogen serves only local energy needs and cannot directly raise blood glucose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Only the liver (and kidney) express glucose-6-phosphatase, which dephosphorylates glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose for export. Skeletal muscle lacks this enzyme, so muscle glycogen serves only local energy needs and cannot directly raise blood glucose.

questions.stem: The reaction shown converts glucose into the product on the right. What is the primary metabolic purpose of adding this phosphate group as the first step of glycolysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It traps glucose inside the cell by making it membrane-impermeant and commits it to metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: It converts glucose into fructose to bypass regulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: It oxidizes glucose to release carbon dioxide immediately

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly generates ATP through substrate-level phosphorylation

choices.label: E
choices.text: It reduces glucose to prepare it for storage as glycogen without further modification

explanation.summary: Hexokinase/glucokinase phosphorylate glucose to glucose 6-phosphate using ATP. The negatively charged phosphate prevents the sugar from crossing the plasma membrane, trapping it in the cell and committing it to glycolysis and other pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hexokinase/glucokinase phosphorylate glucose to glucose 6-phosphate using ATP. The negatively charged phosphate prevents the sugar from crossing the plasma membrane, trapping it in the cell and committing it to glycolysis and other pathways.

questions.stem: Based on the two enzyme-activity curves shown, why does the enzyme with the sigmoidal, right-shifted curve (higher Km) act as a glucose sensor in the liver?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its high affinity lets it work maximally even during hypoglycemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its low affinity means activity rises steeply only when blood glucose is elevated, such as after a meal

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is inhibited by rising glucose, protecting the liver from overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has a fixed activity independent of glucose concentration

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is fully saturated at fasting glucose levels and cannot respond to increases

explanation.summary: Glucokinase has a high Km (low affinity) and is not saturated at normal fasting glucose, so its activity increases substantially only when glucose is high (postprandial). This lets the liver take up and store glucose selectively when it is abundant. Hexokinase, with a low Km, is already saturated and functions during low glucose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucokinase has a high Km (low affinity) and is not saturated at normal fasting glucose, so its activity increases substantially only when glucose is high (postprandial). This lets the liver take up and store glucose selectively when it is abundant. Hexokinase, with a low Km, is already saturated and functions during low glucose.

questions.stem: This double-reciprocal (Lineweaver-Burk) plot compares an enzyme with and without an inhibitor. Given that the two lines share the same y-intercept but the inhibitor line has a steeper slope and a less negative x-intercept, what type of inhibition is depicted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uncompetitive inhibition (decreased Vmax and decreased Km)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition (permanent loss of active enzyme)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allosteric activation (increased Vmax and decreased Km)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Competitive inhibition (unchanged Vmax, increased apparent Km)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Noncompetitive inhibition (decreased Vmax, unchanged Km)

explanation.summary: A shared y-intercept means Vmax is unchanged, while the shifted x-intercept indicates an increased apparent Km. This pattern is classic for competitive inhibition, where the inhibitor competes with substrate and can be overcome by high substrate concentrations.
explanation.whyCorrect: A shared y-intercept means Vmax is unchanged, while the shifted x-intercept indicates an increased apparent Km. This pattern is classic for competitive inhibition, where the inhibitor competes with substrate and can be overcome by high substrate concentrations.

questions.stem: In this double-reciprocal (Lineweaver-Burk) plot, the inhibitor line shares the same x-intercept as the control but has a higher y-intercept. What type of enzyme inhibition does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Feedback activation of the enzyme

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uncompetitive inhibition (decreased Vmax and decreased Km)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Substrate activation (increased Vmax)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Noncompetitive inhibition (decreased Vmax, unchanged Km)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Competitive inhibition (unchanged Vmax, increased Km)

explanation.summary: A shared x-intercept indicates an unchanged Km, while the raised y-intercept reflects a lowered Vmax. This is the signature of noncompetitive inhibition, in which the inhibitor binds independent of substrate and cannot be overcome by adding more substrate.
explanation.whyCorrect: A shared x-intercept indicates an unchanged Km, while the raised y-intercept reflects a lowered Vmax. This is the signature of noncompetitive inhibition, in which the inhibitor binds independent of substrate and cannot be overcome by adding more substrate.

questions.stem: An oxidizing agent acts on the open-chain form of glucose to yield the product shown at right. Which functional-group conversion has occurred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hydroxyl is reduced to an aldehyde

choices.label: B
choices.text: A phosphate is esterified to C6

choices.label: C
choices.text: The C1 aldehyde is oxidized to a carboxylic acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: The C6 alcohol is oxidized to a ketone

choices.label: E
choices.text: An amine is added to C2

explanation.summary: Oxidation of the exposed aldehyde carbonyl at C1 of glucose produces gluconate, a sugar acid; this reducing-sugar reaction underlies assays that detect glucose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oxidation of the exposed aldehyde carbonyl at C1 of glucose produces gluconate, a sugar acid; this reducing-sugar reaction underlies assays that detect glucose.

questions.stem: Two forms of starch are shown: one is a linear chain and the other contains a periodic branch point. What is the functional consequence of the branching linkage that distinguishes amylopectin from amylose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It prevents the molecule from being stored intracellularly

choices.label: B
choices.text: It renders the polymer water-insoluble and structural

choices.label: C
choices.text: It converts the sugar units from glucose to galactose

choices.label: D
choices.text: It makes the polymer indigestible by human enzymes

choices.label: E
choices.text: It creates additional non-reducing ends that speed enzymatic release of glucose

explanation.summary: Amylose is joined only by α-1,4 bonds, while amylopectin adds α-1,6 branch points. Each branch adds a non-reducing terminus, allowing amylases and phosphorylases to mobilize glucose from many ends simultaneously.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amylose is joined only by α-1,4 bonds, while amylopectin adds α-1,6 branch points. Each branch adds a non-reducing terminus, allowing amylases and phosphorylases to mobilize glucose from many ends simultaneously.

questions.stem: This structural polysaccharide of plants is a linear glucose polymer joined by the linkage highlighted at right. Why can humans not derive energy from it despite its being pure glucose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is too highly branched for amylase to access

choices.label: B
choices.text: The glucose units are in the L-configuration and cannot be metabolized

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is covalently bound to protein and cannot be cleaved

choices.label: D
choices.text: Its glucose is permanently oxidized to gluconate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Humans lack an enzyme that can hydrolyze the β-1,4 glycosidic bond

explanation.summary: Cellulose uses β-1,4 linkages; human digestive enzymes (amylases) cleave only α-1,4 bonds, so cellulose passes through as indigestible dietary fiber.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cellulose uses β-1,4 linkages; human digestive enzymes (amylases) cleave only α-1,4 bonds, so cellulose passes through as indigestible dietary fiber.

questions.stem: In this enterocyte, glucose is moved from the intestinal lumen across the apical membrane against its concentration gradient. What directly powers this uphill transport?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The concentration gradient of fructose

choices.label: B
choices.text: The inward Na+ electrochemical gradient maintained by the basolateral Na+/K+ ATPase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct ATP hydrolysis by the apical glucose carrier

choices.label: D
choices.text: GLUT-2 pumping glucose in from blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: Facilitated diffusion driven by glucose alone

explanation.summary: SGLT-1 is a secondary active symporter: it couples glucose uptake to Na+ flowing down the gradient set up by the Na+/K+ ATPase. Glucose then exits basolaterally by facilitated diffusion through GLUT-2.
explanation.whyCorrect: SGLT-1 is a secondary active symporter: it couples glucose uptake to Na+ flowing down the gradient set up by the Na+/K+ ATPase. Glucose then exits basolaterally by facilitated diffusion through GLUT-2.

questions.stem: Glucose and fructose are compared here. They share the molecular formula C6H12O6 but differ in the position of their carbonyl group. What is their structural relationship?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are anomers

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are enantiomers

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are constitutional (structural) isomers

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are identical molecules in different conformations

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are epimers

explanation.summary: Both are hexoses with formula C6H12O6, but glucose carries an aldehyde at C1 and fructose a ketone at C2. Different connectivity of the same atoms makes them constitutional isomers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both are hexoses with formula C6H12O6, but glucose carries an aldehyde at C1 and fructose a ketone at C2. Different connectivity of the same atoms makes them constitutional isomers.

questions.stem: The open-chain sugars at top spontaneously convert to the ring forms at bottom. What type of intramolecular reaction produces these cyclic structures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dehydration to form a double bond

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxidation of the carbonyl to a carboxylic acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peptide bond formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ester formation between two hydroxyls

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hemiacetal/hemiketal formation between the carbonyl and a distant hydroxyl

explanation.summary: An internal hydroxyl (C5 for glucose, C5 for fructose) attacks the carbonyl carbon, forming a cyclic hemiacetal (aldose) or hemiketal (ketose); this creates the new anomeric center marked with an asterisk.
explanation.whyCorrect: An internal hydroxyl (C5 for glucose, C5 for fructose) attacks the carbonyl carbon, forming a cyclic hemiacetal (aldose) or hemiketal (ketose); this creates the new anomeric center marked with an asterisk.

questions.stem: Three common disaccharides are shown. One of them cannot act as a reducing sugar because both anomeric carbons are tied up in its glycosidic bond. Which one is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cellobiose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maltose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isomaltose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sucrose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lactose

explanation.summary: Sucrose links glucose C1 to fructose C2 (α-1,2), engaging both anomeric carbons, so it has no free reducing end. Lactose and maltose each retain a free anomeric carbon and are reducing sugars.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sucrose links glucose C1 to fructose C2 (α-1,2), engaging both anomeric carbons, so it has no free reducing end. Lactose and maltose each retain a free anomeric carbon and are reducing sugars.

questions.stem: The open-chain glucose cyclizes into the two ring forms shown, which differ only in the orientation of the hydroxyl at the newly formed anomeric carbon. What are these two interconverting forms called?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constitutional isomers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diastereomeric aldoses and ketoses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epimers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enantiomers

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anomers

explanation.summary: Ring closure creates a new stereocenter at C1 (the anomeric carbon); the α and β forms differ only in the configuration at that single carbon and are therefore anomers that interconvert by mutarotation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ring closure creates a new stereocenter at C1 (the anomeric carbon); the α and β forms differ only in the configuration at that single carbon and are therefore anomers that interconvert by mutarotation.

questions.stem: This disaccharide joins a six-membered pyranose ring to a five-membered furanose ring through their two anomeric carbons. Which sugar is depicted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maltose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trehalose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sucrose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cellobiose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lactose

explanation.summary: The molecule is a glucopyranose linked to a fructofuranose via an α-1,2 glycosidic bond joining both anomeric carbons, the defining structure of sucrose (table sugar).
explanation.whyCorrect: The molecule is a glucopyranose linked to a fructofuranose via an α-1,2 glycosidic bond joining both anomeric carbons, the defining structure of sucrose (table sugar).

questions.stem: This highly branched storage polymer of glucose uses α-1,4 bonds along its chains and α-1,6 bonds at branch points, with its single reducing end anchored to glycogenin. What molecule is shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amylopectin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glycogen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amylose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cellulose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dextran

explanation.summary: The dense branching pattern with α-1,4 chains, α-1,6 branch points, numerous non-reducing ends, and a reducing end attached to the primer protein glycogenin identifies mammalian glycogen.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dense branching pattern with α-1,4 chains, α-1,6 branch points, numerous non-reducing ends, and a reducing end attached to the primer protein glycogenin identifies mammalian glycogen.

questions.stem: In this pathway, when galactose is present in excess the aldose reductase branch generates a poorly-metabolized sugar alcohol that accumulates. In which tissue does this accumulation most classically cause disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lens of the eye (cataracts)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepatic sinusoids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skeletal muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal proximal tubule

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cardiac myocytes

explanation.summary: The high-Km aldose reductase pathway becomes active when galactose accumulates, converting it to galactitol. Galactitol is osmotically trapped in the lens, drawing in water and producing cataracts, a hallmark of galactokinase deficiency and classic galactosemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The high-Km aldose reductase pathway becomes active when galactose accumulates, converting it to galactitol. Galactitol is osmotically trapped in the lens, drawing in water and producing cataracts, a hallmark of galactokinase deficiency and classic galactosemia.

questions.stem: The diagram shows sequential oxidation of a fat-soluble vitamin. The final active metabolite drives embryonic development, tissue maintenance, and immunity by which primary molecular mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Functioning as a cofactor for gamma-carboxylation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serving as the chromophore for photon absorption in rods

choices.label: C
choices.text: Forming rhodopsin in cone photoreceptors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acting as an enzymatic antioxidant in membranes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Binding nuclear receptors to regulate gene transcription

explanation.summary: Retinol is oxidized by ADH to retinaldehyde (vision, as the visual chromophore) and irreversibly by ALDH to retinoic acid. Retinoic acid binds nuclear RAR/RXR receptors to control gene transcription, driving embryonic development, epithelial maintenance, and immune function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Retinol is oxidized by ADH to retinaldehyde (vision, as the visual chromophore) and irreversibly by ALDH to retinoic acid. Retinoic acid binds nuclear RAR/RXR receptors to control gene transcription, driving embryonic development, epithelial maintenance, and immune function.

questions.stem: This hepatic pathway is upregulated under a low insulin/glucagon ratio and uses unique enzymes to bypass the irreversible steps of glycolysis. What is the primary physiologic purpose of the depicted pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Generating NADPH for reductive biosynthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Synthesizing glucose de novo to maintain blood glucose during fasting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Storing excess glucose as glycogen after a meal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Producing ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oxidizing fatty acids for ketone body production

explanation.summary: The figure depicts gluconeogenesis, using glucose-6-phosphatase, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, PEP carboxykinase, and pyruvate carboxylase to circumvent glycolytic irreversible steps. A low insulin/glucagon ratio (fasting) drives this pathway to keep blood glucose stable.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure depicts gluconeogenesis, using glucose-6-phosphatase, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, PEP carboxykinase, and pyruvate carboxylase to circumvent glycolytic irreversible steps. A low insulin/glucagon ratio (fasting) drives this pathway to keep blood glucose stable.

questions.stem: The figure shows cytosolic NADH from glycolysis being converted into a form that can hand electrons across the mitochondrial membrane to the ETC via a carrier shuttle. Why is such a shuttle system necessary?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NADH is too unstable to exist in the cytosol

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ETC cannot accept electrons from any reduced carrier

choices.label: C
choices.text: NAD+ cannot be regenerated in the cytosol by any means

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycolysis occurs inside the mitochondrial matrix

choices.label: E
choices.text: The inner mitochondrial membrane is impermeable to NADH itself

explanation.summary: Cytosolic NADH generated in glycolysis cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane. Shuttle systems (malate-aspartate or glycerol-3-phosphate) transfer the reducing equivalents into the matrix so electrons reach the ETC while regenerating cytosolic NAD+.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cytosolic NADH generated in glycolysis cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane. Shuttle systems (malate-aspartate or glycerol-3-phosphate) transfer the reducing equivalents into the matrix so electrons reach the ETC while regenerating cytosolic NAD+.

questions.stem: In the shuttle shown, cytosolic electrons are ultimately delivered to the ETC via FAD(2H) rather than NADH. Compared with the malate-aspartate shuttle, what is the consequence for ATP yield?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No ATP is produced because FAD cannot enter the ETC

choices.label: B
choices.text: ATP yield doubles due to two FAD molecules used

choices.label: C
choices.text: More ATP because FADH2 bypasses proton pumping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identical ATP yield since both feed complex I

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fewer ATP per cytosolic NADH because electrons enter at complex II via FADH2

explanation.summary: The glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle transfers electrons to mitochondrial FAD, forming FADH2 that enters the chain at complex II/CoQ. This bypasses complex I, pumping fewer protons and yielding less ATP per cytosolic NADH than the malate-aspartate shuttle.
explanation.whyCorrect: The glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle transfers electrons to mitochondrial FAD, forming FADH2 that enters the chain at complex II/CoQ. This bypasses complex I, pumping fewer protons and yielding less ATP per cytosolic NADH than the malate-aspartate shuttle.

questions.stem: The figure shows the branching fates of a central three-carbon metabolite. Which enzyme step regenerates cytosolic NAD+ under anaerobic conditions to sustain continued glycolysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pyruvate carboxylase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase

explanation.summary: Pyruvate sits at a metabolic crossroads. When oxygen is limited, LDH reduces pyruvate to lactate while oxidizing NADH to NAD+, replenishing the NAD+ needed for glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase so glycolysis can continue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pyruvate sits at a metabolic crossroads. When oxygen is limited, LDH reduces pyruvate to lactate while oxidizing NADH to NAD+, replenishing the NAD+ needed for glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase so glycolysis can continue.

questions.stem: The figure shows covalent regulation of this enzyme complex. A buildup of ATP, acetyl-CoA, and NADH shifts the complex toward its inactive form. What does this signal about the cell's status?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oxygen is absent and anaerobic metabolism dominates

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin is high and glucose uptake is maximal

choices.label: C
choices.text: The cell is energy-replete, so pyruvate oxidation is downregulated

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cell is energy-depleted and needs more acetyl-CoA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcium is low and muscle contraction has ceased

explanation.summary: High ATP, acetyl-CoA, and NADH indicate abundant energy and reducing equivalents. These activate PDH kinase, phosphorylating the complex to its inactive form and slowing conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, sparing pyruvate for gluconeogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: High ATP, acetyl-CoA, and NADH indicate abundant energy and reducing equivalents. These activate PDH kinase, phosphorylating the complex to its inactive form and slowing conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, sparing pyruvate for gluconeogenesis.

questions.stem: The figure divides this pathway into an early phase that consumes ATP and a later phase that generates ATP and NADH. What is the net ATP and NADH yield per glucose molecule shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Net 0 ATP and 2 NADH

choices.label: B
choices.text: Net 4 ATP and 4 NADH

choices.label: C
choices.text: Net 2 ATP and 4 NADH

choices.label: D
choices.text: Net 2 ATP and 2 NADH

choices.label: E
choices.text: Net 6 ATP and 2 NADH

explanation.summary: The investment phase uses 2 ATP; the production phase makes 4 ATP and 2 NADH. The net is therefore 2 ATP and 2 NADH per glucose, along with 2 pyruvate that can feed acetyl-CoA production for the TCA cycle.
explanation.whyCorrect: The investment phase uses 2 ATP; the production phase makes 4 ATP and 2 NADH. The net is therefore 2 ATP and 2 NADH per glucose, along with 2 pyruvate that can feed acetyl-CoA production for the TCA cycle.

questions.stem: The three highlighted steps of this pathway are its irreversible, rate-controlling reactions. Which of these enzymes is the single most important committed rate-limiting step, activated by fructose-2,6-bisphosphate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hexokinase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucokinase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyruvate kinase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphofructokinase-1

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pyruvate carboxylase

explanation.summary: PFK-1 catalyzes the committed, rate-limiting step of glycolysis. It is allosterically activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate and inhibited by ATP and citrate, making it the principal control point of the pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: PFK-1 catalyzes the committed, rate-limiting step of glycolysis. It is allosterically activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate and inhibited by ATP and citrate, making it the principal control point of the pathway.

questions.stem: This pathway is shown as the mechanism behind chronic hyperglycemic tissue damage. In a poorly controlled diabetic patient, which downstream consequence of this pathway is most directly responsible for lens swelling and diabetic cataract formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depletion of NADPH impairing glutathione-mediated antioxidant defense

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accelerated flux through the pentose phosphate pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intracellular sorbitol accumulation drawing water in by osmosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Non-enzymatic glycation of long-lived structural proteins

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased conversion of fructose back to glucose

explanation.summary: In the polyol pathway aldose reductase converts glucose to sorbitol using NADPH; sorbitol is poorly membrane-permeable and osmotically active, so its accumulation (e.g., in the lens) draws in water and causes osmotic stress leading to cataracts. NADPH consumption separately promotes oxidative stress by limiting GSH regeneration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the polyol pathway aldose reductase converts glucose to sorbitol using NADPH; sorbitol is poorly membrane-permeable and osmotically active, so its accumulation (e.g., in the lens) draws in water and causes osmotic stress leading to cataracts. NADPH consumption separately promotes oxidative stress by limiting GSH regeneration.

questions.stem: The figure contrasts the entry of fructose and glucose into hepatic metabolism. Which feature explains why dietary fructose is metabolized more rapidly and less controllably than glucose, driving lipogenesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fructose is phosphorylated by hexokinase with a very high Km

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fructose directly inhibits glucokinase to slow glucose entry

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fructose is trapped as fructose 1-phosphate and cleaved by aldolase B, bypassing the PFK-1 regulatory step

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fructose must first be converted to galactose before entering glycolysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fructose enters cells only via insulin-dependent GLUT4 transporters

explanation.summary: Fructokinase (low Km, high Vmax) rapidly traps fructose as fructose 1-phosphate, which aldolase B splits into triose phosphates downstream of PFK-1. Because it bypasses the rate-limiting PFK-1 control point, fructose flux is unregulated and readily feeds lipogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fructokinase (low Km, high Vmax) rapidly traps fructose as fructose 1-phosphate, which aldolase B splits into triose phosphates downstream of PFK-1. Because it bypasses the rate-limiting PFK-1 control point, fructose flux is unregulated and readily feeds lipogenesis.

questions.stem: The figure compares how glucose and fructose feed into the pathway that generates acetyl-CoA and citrate for lipid synthesis. What is the key significance of fructose entering below the indicated regulatory step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It allows fructose to be stored directly as glycogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: It activates PFK-1 to increase glycolytic control

choices.label: C
choices.text: It slows triglyceride production compared with glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: It escapes PFK-1 negative feedback, aggressively promoting lipogenesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: It prevents citrate from being used for fatty acid synthesis

explanation.summary: Glycolysis is regulated at PFK-1, which is inhibited by ATP/citrate to shunt intermediates elsewhere. Fructose enters distal to PFK-1, so it bypasses this feedback control and continuously supplies carbons for fatty acid and lipid synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycolysis is regulated at PFK-1, which is inhibited by ATP/citrate to shunt intermediates elsewhere. Fructose enters distal to PFK-1, so it bypasses this feedback control and continuously supplies carbons for fatty acid and lipid synthesis.

questions.stem: The figure depicts the protein-mediated exchange occurring between VLDL and HDL particles. What is the net effect of this exchange activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Free fatty acids are shuttled from adipocytes to the liver

choices.label: B
choices.text: LDL receptors are upregulated on hepatocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: HDL transfers triglycerides to VLDL and receives cholesteryl esters

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apolipoprotein B-100 is transferred from VLDL to HDL

choices.label: E
choices.text: HDL gives up cholesteryl esters to VLDL and receives triglycerides in return

explanation.summary: Cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) swaps cholesteryl esters from HDL into VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides moving to HDL. This raises triglyceride content of HDL and delivers cholesteryl esters to apoB-containing particles.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) swaps cholesteryl esters from HDL into VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides moving to HDL. This raises triglyceride content of HDL and delivers cholesteryl esters to apoB-containing particles.

questions.stem: The figure compares the major lipoprotein classes by composition and function. Which particle is the largest, has the highest triglyceride content, and delivers dietary lipids absorbed from the gut?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HDL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chylomicron

choices.label: C
choices.text: LDL

choices.label: D
choices.text: IDL

choices.label: E
choices.text: VLDL

explanation.summary: Chylomicrons are the largest, least dense lipoproteins with the highest triglyceride content; they carry dietary triglycerides from the intestine to peripheral tissues and dietary cholesterol to the liver. HDL is smallest/densest, LDL is cholesterol-rich, and VLDL carries hepatic triglycerides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chylomicrons are the largest, least dense lipoproteins with the highest triglyceride content; they carry dietary triglycerides from the intestine to peripheral tissues and dietary cholesterol to the liver. HDL is smallest/densest, LDL is cholesterol-rich, and VLDL carries hepatic triglycerides.

questions.stem: The figure shows citrate being exported from the mitochondrion and processed in the cytoplasm. What is the primary purpose of this shuttle for a cell performing fatty acid synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To transport fatty acids into the mitochondrion for beta-oxidation

choices.label: B
choices.text: To export urea nitrogen out of the mitochondrion

choices.label: C
choices.text: To regenerate FADH2 for the electron transport chain

choices.label: D
choices.text: To import pyruvate for gluconeogenesis in the matrix

choices.label: E
choices.text: To deliver acetyl-CoA to the cytosol and generate NADPH for fatty acid biosynthesis

explanation.summary: Acetyl-CoA cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane, so it is exported as citrate. Cytosolic citrate lyase regenerates acetyl-CoA for fatty acid synthesis, and the malate/malic enzyme steps produce NADPH needed as reducing power.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acetyl-CoA cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane, so it is exported as citrate. Cytosolic citrate lyase regenerates acetyl-CoA for fatty acid synthesis, and the malate/malic enzyme steps produce NADPH needed as reducing power.

questions.stem: The figure shows the regulation of the committed enzyme of fatty acid synthesis. In a fasting state with high AMP, which regulatory event predominates to switch this enzyme off?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palmitoyl-CoA is removed, relieving inhibition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malonyl-CoA feedback stimulates the enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin-activated phosphatase dephosphorylates and activates the enzyme

choices.label: D
choices.text: AMP-activated protein kinase phosphorylates the enzyme, inactivating it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Citrate allosterically activates the enzyme

explanation.summary: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase is activated by insulin (via phosphatase, dephosphorylated form) and citrate, and inhibited by phosphorylation via AMP-activated protein kinase and by palmitoyl-CoA. During fasting, high AMP activates AMPK, which phosphorylates and inactivates the enzyme to halt fatty acid synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase is activated by insulin (via phosphatase, dephosphorylated form) and citrate, and inhibited by phosphorylation via AMP-activated protein kinase and by palmitoyl-CoA. During fasting, high AMP activates AMPK, which phosphorylates and inactivates the enzyme to halt fatty acid synthesis.

questions.stem: The figure traces the fate of excess dietary glucose in the hepatocyte. What overall process is being depicted, culminating in VLDL secretion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: De novo hepatic lipogenesis and packaging of triglycerides into VLDL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gluconeogenesis from amino acid precursors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycogen synthesis and storage in the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids for ATP

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ketone body synthesis for peripheral fuel

explanation.summary: Excess glucose is metabolized through glycolysis to pyruvate/acetyl-CoA; citrate exports carbons for fatty acid synthesis (fatty acid synthase making palmitate), which are esterified to triglycerides, combined with apolipoproteins, and secreted as VLDL.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess glucose is metabolized through glycolysis to pyruvate/acetyl-CoA; citrate exports carbons for fatty acid synthesis (fatty acid synthase making palmitate), which are esterified to triglycerides, combined with apolipoproteins, and secreted as VLDL.

questions.stem: The figure shows how long-chain fatty acyl groups are moved across the mitochondrial membranes. What is the function of this system and its rate-limiting enzyme?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It imports pyruvate into the matrix for the TCA cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: It exports acetyl-CoA to the cytosol for fatty acid synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It transports long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondrial matrix for beta-oxidation, with carnitine acyltransferase I as the rate-limiting step

choices.label: D
choices.text: It moves cholesterol into the matrix for steroid synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: It shuttles NADH equivalents into the mitochondrion

explanation.summary: Long-chain fatty acyl-CoA is conjugated to carnitine by carnitine acyltransferase I (the rate-limiting, malonyl-CoA-inhibited step) on the outer membrane, translocated across, and reconverted to acyl-CoA by carnitine acyltransferase II in the matrix for beta-oxidation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Long-chain fatty acyl-CoA is conjugated to carnitine by carnitine acyltransferase I (the rate-limiting, malonyl-CoA-inhibited step) on the outer membrane, translocated across, and reconverted to acyl-CoA by carnitine acyltransferase II in the matrix for beta-oxidation.

questions.stem: In this diagram, a molecule produced during active fatty acid synthesis blocks the enzyme shuttling long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondrion. What is the physiologic purpose of this inhibition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It prevents simultaneous synthesis and oxidation of fatty acids (futile cycling)

choices.label: B
choices.text: It accelerates ketone body production in the fed state

choices.label: C
choices.text: It promotes carnitine synthesis in the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: It activates beta-oxidation when insulin is high

choices.label: E
choices.text: It depletes cytosolic acetyl-CoA to halt gluconeogenesis

explanation.summary: Malonyl-CoA, the committed intermediate of fatty acid synthesis, inhibits CPT I. This stops newly made fatty acids from being carried into the mitochondrion for beta-oxidation, preventing a wasteful synthesis/degradation cycle during the fed state.
explanation.whyCorrect: Malonyl-CoA, the committed intermediate of fatty acid synthesis, inhibits CPT I. This stops newly made fatty acids from being carried into the mitochondrion for beta-oxidation, preventing a wasteful synthesis/degradation cycle during the fed state.

questions.stem: The reaction shown transfers a fatty acyl group onto the hydroxyl of a sterol. What is the main functional consequence of this modification?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It converts the sterol into a bile acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: It converts the sterol into a nonpolar form suited for storage and lipoprotein transport

choices.label: C
choices.text: It activates the sterol as a steroid hormone

choices.label: D
choices.text: It targets the sterol for lysosomal degradation

choices.label: E
choices.text: It makes the sterol water-soluble for renal excretion

explanation.summary: ACAT esterifies cholesterol's free 3-OH with a fatty acyl group, producing cholesterol ester. Esterification removes the polar hydroxyl, yielding a more hydrophobic molecule ideal for storage and packaging into lipoprotein cores.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACAT esterifies cholesterol's free 3-OH with a fatty acyl group, producing cholesterol ester. Esterification removes the polar hydroxyl, yielding a more hydrophobic molecule ideal for storage and packaging into lipoprotein cores.

questions.stem: This pathway is confined to hepatic mitochondria because one of its enzymes is expressed almost exclusively in the liver. Which enzyme provides this tissue specificity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thiolase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase

choices.label: D
choices.text: HMG-CoA synthase (mitochondrial)

choices.label: E
choices.text: HMG-CoA lyase

explanation.summary: The diagram depicts ketogenesis. Mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase, which is essentially liver-specific, commits the pathway, which is why the liver is the principal site of ketone body production.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram depicts ketogenesis. Mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase, which is essentially liver-specific, commits the pathway, which is why the liver is the principal site of ketone body production.

questions.stem: This pathway lets peripheral tissues burn ketone bodies but cannot occur in the liver. Which feature explains why the liver cannot use the ketones it makes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The liver lacks HMG-CoA synthase

choices.label: B
choices.text: The liver lacks beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase (thiophorase)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The liver lacks a functional TCA cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: The liver lacks beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase

choices.label: E
choices.text: The liver lacks thiolase

explanation.summary: Ketolysis requires beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase (thiophorase/SCOT) to activate acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA. The liver does not express this enzyme, so it exports ketone bodies for use by extrahepatic tissues rather than consuming them itself.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ketolysis requires beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase (thiophorase/SCOT) to activate acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA. The liver does not express this enzyme, so it exports ketone bodies for use by extrahepatic tissues rather than consuming them itself.

questions.stem: The hormone shown exerts negative control at several steps in adipose tissue and liver. What is the net effect of high levels of this hormone on ketone body production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It promotes ketone synthesis by activating CPT I

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has no effect on ketone production

choices.label: C
choices.text: It suppresses ketogenesis by inhibiting lipolysis and hepatic fatty acid oxidation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It stimulates ketogenesis by driving lipolysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: It increases ketogenesis by inhibiting gluconeogenesis

explanation.summary: Insulin inhibits adipocyte triglyceride breakdown, reduces the flux of free fatty acids to the liver, and blocks hepatic ketogenesis. Thus a high insulin (fed) state strongly suppresses ketone body formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Insulin inhibits adipocyte triglyceride breakdown, reduces the flux of free fatty acids to the liver, and blocks hepatic ketogenesis. Thus a high insulin (fed) state strongly suppresses ketone body formation.

questions.stem: Excess metabolic acids consume bicarbonate and drive the buffer equilibrium shown. What is the compensatory response depicted at the far right, and by what mechanism does it help?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deep rapid (Kussmaul) breathing blows off CO2, shifting the equilibrium to consume H+

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vomiting to remove metabolic acids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal bicarbonate excretion to lower pH

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shallow slow breathing to retain CO2 and raise pH

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperventilation that raises PaCO2

explanation.summary: In metabolic acidosis, accumulating acids lower bicarbonate and pH. Respiratory compensation is Kussmaul breathing: hyperventilation exhales CO2, pulling the H2CO3/CO2 equilibrium rightward and consuming H+, which partially raises pH.
explanation.whyCorrect: In metabolic acidosis, accumulating acids lower bicarbonate and pH. Respiratory compensation is Kussmaul breathing: hyperventilation exhales CO2, pulling the H2CO3/CO2 equilibrium rightward and consuming H+, which partially raises pH.

questions.stem: This overview shows the liver making circulating fuel from fatty acids during fasting for delivery to heart, muscle, and brain. What is the key metabolic benefit of this system during prolonged starvation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It supplies an alternative fuel that spares glucose for the brain and red blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: It provides fuel that red blood cells can oxidize instead of glucose

choices.label: C
choices.text: It allows the liver to consume its own ketones for energy

choices.label: D
choices.text: It replaces the need for gluconeogenesis entirely

choices.label: E
choices.text: It converts fatty acids directly into glucose

explanation.summary: During fasting, low insulin/high glucagon drives lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis. Ketone bodies circulate as an alternative acetyl-CoA source for the brain and muscle, sparing glucose for tissues (RBCs, and the brain early on) that depend on it.
explanation.whyCorrect: During fasting, low insulin/high glucagon drives lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis. Ketone bodies circulate as an alternative acetyl-CoA source for the brain and muscle, sparing glucose for tissues (RBCs, and the brain early on) that depend on it.

questions.stem: This diagram shows the cleavage sites of four phospholipases on a membrane phospholipid. Cleavage by the enzyme acting on the glycerophosphate bond (phospholipase C) generates which pair of intracellular second messengers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diacylglycerol and IP3

choices.label: B
choices.text: cAMP and cGMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arachidonic acid and a lysophospholipid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ceramide and sphingosine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phosphatidic acid and choline

explanation.summary: Phospholipase C cleaves the bond between glycerol and the phosphorylated head group. Acting on PIP2 it yields membrane-bound diacylglycerol (activates PKC) and soluble IP3 (releases ER calcium).
explanation.whyCorrect: Phospholipase C cleaves the bond between glycerol and the phosphorylated head group. Acting on PIP2 it yields membrane-bound diacylglycerol (activates PKC) and soluble IP3 (releases ER calcium).

questions.stem: What is this 20-carbon polyunsaturated fatty acid, the precursor cleaved from membrane phospholipids to make eicosanoids?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Linoleic acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eicosapentaenoic acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Palmitic acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prostaglandin E2

choices.label: E
choices.text: Arachidonic acid

explanation.summary: Arachidonic acid is a 20-carbon fatty acid with four cis double bonds. Released by phospholipase A2, it is the substrate for cyclooxygenases and lipoxygenases that generate prostaglandins, thromboxanes and leukotrienes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arachidonic acid is a 20-carbon fatty acid with four cis double bonds. Released by phospholipase A2, it is the substrate for cyclooxygenases and lipoxygenases that generate prostaglandins, thromboxanes and leukotrienes.

questions.stem: These two panels contrast how hydrophilic and lipophilic hormones act on a target cell. Why does the lipophilic hormone (right) bind an intracellular receptor rather than a surface receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is degraded by enzymes on the cell surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: It requires a membrane transporter to enter the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: It binds only to carrier proteins in the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is too large to fit any surface receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: It can diffuse directly across the plasma membrane

explanation.summary: Lipophilic hormones (e.g., steroids, thyroid hormone) cross the lipid bilayer and bind intracellular/nuclear receptors that act as transcription factors. Hydrophilic hormones cannot cross and must signal through surface receptors and second messengers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lipophilic hormones (e.g., steroids, thyroid hormone) cross the lipid bilayer and bind intracellular/nuclear receptors that act as transcription factors. Hydrophilic hormones cannot cross and must signal through surface receptors and second messengers.

questions.stem: Epinephrine or glucagon binding to this receptor triggers exchange of GDP for GTP on the associated G protein. What is the immediate downstream effect depicted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hydrolysis of cyclic AMP to AMP

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activation of phospholipase C to make IP3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of adenylyl cyclase to produce cyclic AMP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct autophosphorylation of the receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Opening of a ligand-gated ion channel

explanation.summary: This is a Gs-coupled receptor: agonist binding activates the Gs protein (GTP for GDP exchange), the alpha subunit dissociates and stimulates adenylyl cyclase, raising cyclic AMP as the second messenger.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is a Gs-coupled receptor: agonist binding activates the Gs protein (GTP for GDP exchange), the alpha subunit dissociates and stimulates adenylyl cyclase, raising cyclic AMP as the second messenger.
