questions.stem: This cascade is initiated when receptor-mediated signals convert Ras-GDP to active Ras-GTP. What is the ultimate result of the ensuing Raf to MEK to MAP kinase to Erk relay?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Release of neurotransmitter vesicles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activation of gene transcription in the nucleus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Degradation of cyclic AMP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opening of voltage-gated calcium channels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of DNA replication

explanation.summary: Active Ras-GTP triggers a phosphorylation cascade (Raf, MEK, MAP kinase, Erk). Phosphorylated Erk translocates to the nucleus and activates transcription factors, driving genes for growth and proliferation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Active Ras-GTP triggers a phosphorylation cascade (Raf, MEK, MAP kinase, Erk). Phosphorylated Erk translocates to the nucleus and activates transcription factors, driving genes for growth and proliferation.

questions.stem: This four-step figure shows a receptor tyrosine kinase responding to its ligand. What activating event occurs immediately after ligand-induced dimerization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Internalization and immediate degradation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues

choices.label: C
choices.text: GTP hydrolysis by an associated G protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proteolytic cleavage of the receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Opening of an intrinsic ion pore

explanation.summary: Ligand binding brings two RTK monomers together; dimerization juxtaposes their kinase domains so they cross-phosphorylate each other's tyrosines. These phosphotyrosines then recruit intracellular signaling proteins.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ligand binding brings two RTK monomers together; dimerization juxtaposes their kinase domains so they cross-phosphorylate each other's tyrosines. These phosphotyrosines then recruit intracellular signaling proteins.

questions.stem: Insulin binding triggers tyrosine phosphorylation that branches into two intracellular pathways here. Which outcome is mediated by the phosphoinositide-3 kinase branch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cell growth and DNA synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Net degradation of glycogen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stimulation of gluconeogenesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of protein synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Translocation of GLUT4 vesicles to import glucose

explanation.summary: The PI3-kinase branch of insulin signaling drives GLUT4 vesicle translocation (glucose uptake) plus glycogen, lipid and protein synthesis. The RAS/MAP kinase branch handles the growth/DNA synthesis outcomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PI3-kinase branch of insulin signaling drives GLUT4 vesicle translocation (glucose uptake) plus glycogen, lipid and protein synthesis. The RAS/MAP kinase branch handles the growth/DNA synthesis outcomes.

questions.stem: In this insulin pathway, activated PI 3-kinase generates PI-3,4,5-trisphosphate in the inner membrane leaflet. What is the role of this lipid product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It directly phosphorylates the insulin receptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is cleaved to form IP3 and DAG

choices.label: C
choices.text: It opens calcium channels as a diffusible second messenger

choices.label: D
choices.text: It provides a PH-domain docking site that recruits and activates PKB

choices.label: E
choices.text: It inhibits PDK1 to end the signal

explanation.summary: PI-3,4,5-trisP recruits pleckstrin-homology (PH) domain proteins such as PDK1 and PKB (Akt) to the membrane. Co-localization lets PDK1 phosphorylate and activate PKB, which then dissociates to signal.
explanation.whyCorrect: PI-3,4,5-trisP recruits pleckstrin-homology (PH) domain proteins such as PDK1 and PKB (Akt) to the membrane. Co-localization lets PDK1 phosphorylate and activate PKB, which then dissociates to signal.

questions.stem: Fatty acids and eicosanoids in this figure act as ligands for PPARalpha and PPARgamma, which then heterodimerize with RXR on DNA. What class of signaling molecule are PPARs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ligand-activated nuclear transcription factors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Membrane ion channels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Receptor tyrosine kinases

choices.label: D
choices.text: G protein-coupled receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cytoplasmic second messengers

explanation.summary: PPARs are nuclear receptors: lipophilic ligands (fatty acids, eicosanoids) bind them intracellularly, they pair with RXR, bind response elements on DNA, and regulate transcription of lipid-metabolism genes.
explanation.whyCorrect: PPARs are nuclear receptors: lipophilic ligands (fatty acids, eicosanoids) bind them intracellularly, they pair with RXR, bind response elements on DNA, and regulate transcription of lipid-metabolism genes.

questions.stem: This scheme shows phospholipase C cleaving PI-4,5-bisphosphate to release diacylglycerol (DAG). What is the principal downstream action of the DAG produced?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phosphorylation of receptor tyrosine residues

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct activation of a GPCR

choices.label: C
choices.text: Release of calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of adenylyl cyclase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of protein kinase C

explanation.summary: PLC splits PIP2 into DAG (stays in the membrane) and IP3 (soluble). DAG, together with Ca2+, activates protein kinase C, while IP3 mobilizes calcium from the ER.
explanation.whyCorrect: PLC splits PIP2 into DAG (stays in the membrane) and IP3 (soluble). DAG, together with Ca2+, activates protein kinase C, while IP3 mobilizes calcium from the ER.

questions.stem: In contracting skeletal muscle, a nerve impulse raises intracellular Ca2+, which binds calmodulin and drives the enzyme cascade shown. What is the physiological purpose of coupling this Ca2+ signal to the cascade?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To pump calcium back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: B
choices.text: To trigger muscle protein synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: To relax the muscle after contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: To mobilize glucose from glycogen to fuel muscle contraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: To synthesize new glycogen stores

explanation.summary: The same Ca2+ signal that triggers contraction also activates calmodulin/phosphorylase kinase, which phosphorylates and activates glycogen phosphorylase. This links energy demand to glycogen breakdown, releasing glucose to power the working muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: The same Ca2+ signal that triggers contraction also activates calmodulin/phosphorylase kinase, which phosphorylates and activates glycogen phosphorylase. This links energy demand to glycogen breakdown, releasing glucose to power the working muscle.

questions.stem: In the pathway depicted, the signaling molecule diffuses from an endothelial or nerve cell into an adjacent smooth muscle cell, where it binds guanylate cyclase to convert GTP to cGMP. What is the physiologic end result of this cascade?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac myocyte hypertrophy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smooth muscle contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelet aggregation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased intracellular calcium release

explanation.summary: Nitric oxide (NO) is a gaseous signaling molecule synthesized from arginine and O2. It diffuses into smooth muscle, activates soluble guanylate cyclase to raise cGMP, and the cGMP triggers smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitric oxide (NO) is a gaseous signaling molecule synthesized from arginine and O2. It diffuses into smooth muscle, activates soluble guanylate cyclase to raise cGMP, and the cGMP triggers smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a hormone-bound seven-transmembrane receptor dissociating into subunits that branch into several downstream effectors. Which second messenger cascade is generated specifically by the alpha-q effector branch shown feeding phospholipase C?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DAG and IP3, raising intracellular calcium and activating PKC

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased cAMP via adenylate cyclase inhibition

choices.label: C
choices.text: RhoA activation driving cytoskeletal remodeling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased cAMP activating PKA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct opening of potassium channels causing hyperpolarization

explanation.summary: This is a G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) signaling map. The Gq alpha subunit activates phospholipase C (PLC), which cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3; DAG activates PKC and IP3 releases stored calcium. Gs raises cAMP, Gi lowers it, and G12/13 acts through RhoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is a G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) signaling map. The Gq alpha subunit activates phospholipase C (PLC), which cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3; DAG activates PKC and IP3 releases stored calcium. Gs raises cAMP, Gi lowers it, and G12/13 acts through RhoA.

questions.stem: Panel C of this figure shows a hormone being secreted into a blood vessel by a gland and then acting on distant target cells. Which mode of intercellular communication does this panel illustrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct contact (juxtacrine) signaling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paracrine signaling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endocrine signaling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Synaptic signaling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autocrine signaling

explanation.summary: The four panels contrast modes of cell signaling. Endocrine signaling (panel C) uses hormones released into the bloodstream to reach distant targets, unlike paracrine (local diffusion to neighbors), synaptic (neurotransmitter across a synapse), or direct contact between adjacent plasma membranes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The four panels contrast modes of cell signaling. Endocrine signaling (panel C) uses hormones released into the bloodstream to reach distant targets, unlike paracrine (local diffusion to neighbors), synaptic (neurotransmitter across a synapse), or direct contact between adjacent plasma membranes.

questions.stem: This figure classifies hormones by solubility. Based on the properties shown, how do the lipid-soluble hormones on the right (e.g., cortisol, thyroxine) most likely exert their effect on a target cell compared with the water-soluble hormones on the left?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They bind cell-surface receptors and require a second messenger

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are stored in synaptic vesicles and released by exocytosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: They cannot enter cells and act only on membrane ion channels

choices.label: D
choices.text: They act exclusively through gap junctions between adjacent cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: They diffuse across the plasma membrane and bind intracellular/nuclear receptors to alter gene transcription

explanation.summary: Lipid-soluble (hydrophobic) hormones such as steroids and thyroxine cross the plasma membrane and bind intracellular or nuclear receptors, directly modulating gene transcription. Water-soluble (hydrophilic) hormones like insulin and epinephrine bind surface receptors and act via second messengers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lipid-soluble (hydrophobic) hormones such as steroids and thyroxine cross the plasma membrane and bind intracellular or nuclear receptors, directly modulating gene transcription. Water-soluble (hydrophilic) hormones like insulin and epinephrine bind surface receptors and act via second messengers.

questions.stem: In the pathway shown, a ligand binds the Frizzled receptor and activates Dishevelled, which inhibits the destruction complex containing GSK-3-beta, CKI-alpha, Axin, and APC. What is the direct consequence of inhibiting this destruction complex?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calcium is released to activate PKC

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-catenin is phosphorylated and degraded by the proteasome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-catenin is stabilized, accumulates, and translocates to the nucleus to drive target gene transcription

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Frizzled receptor is internalized and recycled

choices.label: E
choices.text: STAT proteins are dimerized and translocated

explanation.summary: This is the canonical Wnt/beta-catenin pathway. Normally the destruction complex phosphorylates beta-catenin for degradation. When Wnt activates Dishevelled and inhibits the complex, beta-catenin escapes degradation, accumulates, enters the nucleus, and partners with transcription factors to activate target genes.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is the canonical Wnt/beta-catenin pathway. Normally the destruction complex phosphorylates beta-catenin for degradation. When Wnt activates Dishevelled and inhibits the complex, beta-catenin escapes degradation, accumulates, enters the nucleus, and partners with transcription factors to activate target genes.

questions.stem: In this diagram, UV light causes DNA damage in a keratinocyte, activating p53 and POMC to release a peptide that binds MC1R on an adjacent melanocyte, raising cAMP and activating MITF. What is the ultimate protective outcome of this keratinocyte-melanocyte cross-talk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased melanin synthesis and skin lightening

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased melanin pigment production and transfer of melanosomes to protect against further UV damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keratinocyte proliferation and thickening of the stratum corneum only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apoptosis of the melanocyte

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasodilation of dermal blood vessels

explanation.summary: UV-induced DNA damage drives p53-dependent POMC processing to alpha-MSH, which binds melanocyte MC1R. The resulting cAMP/MITF signaling upregulates melanin synthesis, and melanosomes are transferred to keratinocytes, shielding nuclei from further UV damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: UV-induced DNA damage drives p53-dependent POMC processing to alpha-MSH, which binds melanocyte MC1R. The resulting cAMP/MITF signaling upregulates melanin synthesis, and melanosomes are transferred to keratinocytes, shielding nuclei from further UV damage.

questions.stem: The numbered steps show cytokine-induced receptor dimerization, phosphorylation of tyrosine residues by receptor-associated kinases, recruitment and phosphorylation of latent cytoplasmic proteins, their dimerization, and nuclear translocation to bind promoter sequences. Which signaling pathway is illustrated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: JAK-STAT pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: GPCR-cAMP-PKA pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wnt/beta-catenin pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: TGF-beta/SMAD pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Receptor tyrosine kinase-Ras-MAPK pathway

explanation.summary: Cytokine binding dimerizes the receptor, activating associated Janus kinases (JAK) that phosphorylate receptor tyrosines. STAT proteins are recruited, phosphorylated, dimerize, translocate to the nucleus, and bind promoter STAT-binding sequences to drive transcription of cytokine-responsive genes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cytokine binding dimerizes the receptor, activating associated Janus kinases (JAK) that phosphorylate receptor tyrosines. STAT proteins are recruited, phosphorylated, dimerize, translocate to the nucleus, and bind promoter STAT-binding sequences to drive transcription of cytokine-responsive genes.

questions.stem: This illustration contrasts the fate of the alpha subunit under normoxia versus hypoxia. Under normoxic conditions, proline hydroxylation allows VHL to bind the subunit. What is the direct consequence of this VHL binding shown in the top pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cleavage of the subunit into active peptides that promote angiogenesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stabilization and nuclear translocation to activate target genes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ubiquitination of the alpha subunit and its proteasomal degradation

choices.label: D

choices.label: E

explanation.summary: In normoxia, prolyl hydroxylase (PHD) uses O2 to hydroxylate HIF-alpha, allowing von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) protein to bind and target it for ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. In hypoxia, hydroxylation fails, HIF-alpha is stabilized, dimerizes with HIF-beta, and activates genes for erythropoiesis, angiogenesis, and metabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: In normoxia, prolyl hydroxylase (PHD) uses O2 to hydroxylate HIF-alpha, allowing von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) protein to bind and target it for ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. In hypoxia, hydroxylation fails, HIF-alpha is stabilized, dimerizes with HIF-beta, and activates genes for erythropoiesis, angiogenesis, and metabolism.

questions.stem: In this pathway, TNF-alpha binds TNFR-1 and activates the IKK complex, which phosphorylates the inhibitory protein bound to the p50/p65 complex. What must happen to that phosphorylated inhibitory protein before the transcription factor can enter the nucleus and activate immune response genes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is ubiquitinated and degraded by the proteasome, freeing the complex to translocate

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is dephosphorylated and re-binds the complex more tightly

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is transported into the nucleus together with the complex

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is exported from the cell by exocytosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is cleaved to generate an active second messenger

explanation.summary: This is the NF-kB pathway. IKK phosphorylates IkB, marking it for polyubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. Freed p50/p65 (NF-kB) then translocates to the nucleus to activate immune and inflammatory response genes.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is the NF-kB pathway. IKK phosphorylates IkB, marking it for polyubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. Freed p50/p65 (NF-kB) then translocates to the nucleus to activate immune and inflammatory response genes.

questions.stem: This diagram shows glycerol metabolism, with glycerol kinase activity restricted to one organ. Why can glycerol released from adipose lipolysis be converted to glucose only after processing in the liver?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only the liver expresses glycerol kinase to phosphorylate glycerol to glycerol 3-phosphate for gluconeogenesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glycerol must first be reduced to glucose by glycerol 3-phosphate dehydrogenase in muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skeletal muscle exports glycerol 3-phosphate directly into blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the kidney can convert glycerol into dihydroxyacetone phosphate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adipocytes phosphorylate glycerol but lack gluconeogenic enzymes

explanation.summary: Adipose tissue lacks glycerol kinase, so glycerol released during lipolysis travels to the liver, the only major site of glycerol kinase. There it is phosphorylated to glycerol 3-phosphate, which can feed gluconeogenesis (via DHAP) when glucose is scarce or triglyceride synthesis when glucose is abundant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adipose tissue lacks glycerol kinase, so glycerol released during lipolysis travels to the liver, the only major site of glycerol kinase. There it is phosphorylated to glycerol 3-phosphate, which can feed gluconeogenesis (via DHAP) when glucose is scarce or triglyceride synthesis when glucose is abundant.

questions.stem: This figure summarizes the coordinated action of one hormone across liver, muscle, and adipose tissue in the fed state (glycogen synthesis, protein synthesis, and triglyceride storage all promoted). Which hormone drives this integrated anabolic response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glucagon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Epinephrine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin

choices.label: D

choices.label: E
choices.text: Growth hormone

explanation.summary: The figure depicts the fed-state anabolic program: hepatic glycogen and fatty acid synthesis, muscle glucose uptake via GLUT4 with glycogen and protein synthesis, and adipocyte triglyceride storage. This coordinated fuel-storage response is the signature of insulin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure depicts the fed-state anabolic program: hepatic glycogen and fatty acid synthesis, muscle glucose uptake via GLUT4 with glycogen and protein synthesis, and adipocyte triglyceride storage. This coordinated fuel-storage response is the signature of insulin.

questions.stem: In this figure, the liver and adipose tissue mobilize fuels while skeletal muscle shows no response due to lack of receptors. Which hormone produces this pattern?

choices.text: Thyroxine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucagon

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epinephrine

choices.label: E

explanation.summary: Skeletal muscle lacks glucagon receptors, so it does not respond to glucagon. Glucagon acts mainly on the liver (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis) and adipose tissue (lipolysis) to raise blood glucose during fasting, exactly the selective pattern shown.
explanation.whyCorrect: Skeletal muscle lacks glucagon receptors, so it does not respond to glucagon. Glucagon acts mainly on the liver (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis) and adipose tissue (lipolysis) to raise blood glucose during fasting, exactly the selective pattern shown.

questions.stem: In this adipocyte, glucagon acts through cAMP/PKA and insulin acts through PI3K/PKB and protein phosphatase to control the enzyme shown at the center of the cycle. What is the net effect of insulin signaling on hormone-sensitive lipase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insulin raises cAMP to activate PKA and hormone-sensitive lipase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin blocks GLUT4 so glycerol 3-phosphate cannot be made

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin phosphorylates hormone-sensitive lipase to activate lipolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insulin activates lipoprotein lipase to release intracellular glycerol

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insulin activates protein phosphatase, keeping hormone-sensitive lipase dephosphorylated and inactive to suppress lipolysis

explanation.summary: Glucagon raises cAMP and activates PKA, which phosphorylates and activates hormone-sensitive lipase to break TG into glycerol and fatty acids. Insulin opposes this by activating protein phosphatase, dephosphorylating and inactivating the lipase, so lipolysis is suppressed and fat is stored.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucagon raises cAMP and activates PKA, which phosphorylates and activates hormone-sensitive lipase to break TG into glycerol and fatty acids. Insulin opposes this by activating protein phosphatase, dephosphorylating and inactivating the lipase, so lipolysis is suppressed and fat is stored.

questions.stem: This figure contrasts how insulin and glucagon regulate glycogen synthase and phosphorylase kinase via phosphorylation state. Why does phosphorylation by glucagon's pathway have OPPOSITE functional consequences for these two enzymes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phosphorylation activates both enzymes, doubling glycogen synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both enzymes are inactivated by phosphorylation, halting all glycogen turnover

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phosphorylation is only possible on glycogen synthase, not on kinases

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphorylation inactivates glycogen synthase but activates the catabolic enzymes, so one signal simultaneously stops synthesis and promotes degradation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insulin and glucagon phosphorylate the same residue with identical effects

explanation.summary: Reciprocal regulation lets a single covalent modification switch the cell between storage and breakdown. Glucagon (cAMP/PKA) phosphorylates glycogen synthase to inactivate it (stopping synthesis) while phosphorylating phosphorylase kinase to activate the cascade that turns on glycogen phosphorylase (promoting degradation). Insulin's protein phosphatase reverses both.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reciprocal regulation lets a single covalent modification switch the cell between storage and breakdown. Glucagon (cAMP/PKA) phosphorylates glycogen synthase to inactivate it (stopping synthesis) while phosphorylating phosphorylase kinase to activate the cascade that turns on glycogen phosphorylase (promoting degradation). Insulin's protein phosphatase reverses both.

questions.stem: This figure shows fuel mobilization from liver, muscle, and adipose plus stimulated glucagon release and inhibited insulin release, occurring irrespective of blood glucose. Which hormone produces this acute stress response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Epinephrine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucagon

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cortisol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insulin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aldosterone

explanation.summary: Epinephrine mobilizes fuels during acute stress regardless of blood glucose: it drives hepatic and muscle glycogenolysis, adipose lipolysis, and shifts pancreatic islet output toward glucagon while suppressing insulin. Note muscle glycogen yields pyruvate/lactate (muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase), a hallmark distinguishing it from a purely glucose-exporting response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epinephrine mobilizes fuels during acute stress regardless of blood glucose: it drives hepatic and muscle glycogenolysis, adipose lipolysis, and shifts pancreatic islet output toward glucagon while suppressing insulin. Note muscle glycogen yields pyruvate/lactate (muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase), a hallmark distinguishing it from a purely glucose-exporting response.

questions.stem: Each row (A-D) shows a pattern of up/down arrows for insulin:glucagon ratio, hormone-sensitive lipase activity, phosphorylated glycogen synthase, circulating chylomicrons, and hepatic PFK-2 kinase activity. Which row best represents an overnight (post-absorptive) fasting state?

choices.label: A
choices.text: None of the rows is consistent with fasting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Row B: low insulin:glucagon ratio, high HSL activity, high phosphorylated glycogen synthase, low chylomicrons, low PFK-2 kinase activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Row C: high insulin:glucagon ratio, high HSL, low phosphorylated glycogen synthase, high chylomicrons, low PFK-2 kinase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Row A: high insulin:glucagon ratio, low HSL, low phosphorylated glycogen synthase, high chylomicrons, high PFK-2 kinase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Row D: low insulin:glucagon ratio, low HSL, high phosphorylated glycogen synthase, high chylomicrons, low PFK-2 kinase

explanation.summary: During an overnight fast insulin falls and glucagon rises (low ratio). Glucagon/PKA activates hormone-sensitive lipase and phosphorylates glycogen synthase (inactivating it). Dietary fat is absent so chylomicrons fall, and PFK-2 kinase activity is inhibited (phosphatase favored), slowing hepatic glycolysis. Only row B shows all of these trends.
explanation.whyCorrect: During an overnight fast insulin falls and glucagon rises (low ratio). Glucagon/PKA activates hormone-sensitive lipase and phosphorylates glycogen synthase (inactivating it). Dietary fat is absent so chylomicrons fall, and PFK-2 kinase activity is inhibited (phosphatase favored), slowing hepatic glycolysis. Only row B shows all of these trends.

questions.stem: This diagram shows glycogen phosphorylase in skeletal muscle being switched from inactive to active by three inputs: AMP, Ca2+-calmodulin from nerve impulse, and epinephrine via cAMP/PKA. What does the convergence of these signals accomplish during muscle contraction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It exports glucose from muscle into the blood to raise systemic glucose

choices.label: B
choices.text: It couples energy demand and neural/hormonal signals to activate glycogenolysis, supplying glucose for ATP generation

choices.label: C
choices.text: It converts glycogen phosphorylase into glycogen synthase

choices.label: D
choices.text: It blocks glycolysis so lactate cannot accumulate

choices.label: E
choices.text: It inactivates glycogen phosphorylase to conserve glycogen during rest

explanation.summary: Rising AMP (energy depletion), Ca2+-calmodulin (from the nerve impulse triggering contraction), and epinephrine (cAMP/PKA to phosphorylase kinase) all activate glycogen phosphorylase. This ensures glycogenolysis matches contractile energy demand, releasing glucose-1-phosphate for glycolysis within the muscle; insulin promotes the reverse (inactivation).
explanation.whyCorrect: Rising AMP (energy depletion), Ca2+-calmodulin (from the nerve impulse triggering contraction), and epinephrine (cAMP/PKA to phosphorylase kinase) all activate glycogen phosphorylase. This ensures glycogenolysis matches contractile energy demand, releasing glucose-1-phosphate for glycolysis within the muscle; insulin promotes the reverse (inactivation).

questions.stem: The enzyme shown catalyzes a reversible reaction between glycerol and glycerol 3-phosphate. Under which hormonal condition is the RIGHTWARD (glycerol 3-phosphate-forming) direction favored?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated glycolytic flux in muscle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucagon-dominant fasting state with high ATP from fatty-acid oxidation

choices.label: C
choices.text: High circulating DHAP with low ATP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postprandial hyperglycemia with insulin surge

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insulin-dominant fed state

explanation.summary: Glycerol kinase drives glycerol toward glycerol 3-phosphate when glucagon predominates and ATP is high from beta-oxidation; hormone-sensitive lipase releases glycerol from adipocyte triglyceride, pushing the reaction rightward. Insulin and high DHAP favor the reverse (glycerol-forming) direction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycerol kinase drives glycerol toward glycerol 3-phosphate when glucagon predominates and ATP is high from beta-oxidation; hormone-sensitive lipase releases glycerol from adipocyte triglyceride, pushing the reaction rightward. Insulin and high DHAP favor the reverse (glycerol-forming) direction.

questions.stem: The reaction depicted converts two ADP into AMP plus ATP. Why is the AMP produced physiologically important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: AMP is a high-energy phosphate donor for kinases

choices.label: B
choices.text: AMP is the direct energy currency spent by ATPases

choices.label: C
choices.text: A small rise in AMP is a sensitive low-energy signal that activates enzymes like PFK-1 and AMPK

choices.label: D
choices.text: AMP inhibits glycolysis to conserve glucose

choices.label: E
choices.text: AMP signals an energy-replete state and promotes storage

explanation.summary: Because ATP concentrations are large and AMP concentrations are tiny, adenylate kinase amplifies energy deficits: a modest ATP drop yields a large proportional AMP increase, making AMP a sensitive low-energy sensor that stimulates PFK-1, glycogen phosphorylase, and AMPK.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because ATP concentrations are large and AMP concentrations are tiny, adenylate kinase amplifies energy deficits: a modest ATP drop yields a large proportional AMP increase, making AMP a sensitive low-energy sensor that stimulates PFK-1, glycogen phosphorylase, and AMPK.

questions.stem: The graph compares adenine nucleotide concentrations in muscle at rest versus exercise. What overall pattern does exercise produce?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ATP rises sharply while ADP and AMP fall

choices.label: B
choices.text: ATP falls modestly while ADP and AMP rise, signaling energy demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP, ADP, and AMP all rise proportionally

choices.label: D
choices.text: All three nucleotides remain essentially unchanged

choices.label: E
choices.text: AMP falls while ATP and ADP both increase

explanation.summary: During exercise, ATP is buffered and only drops modestly, but the small absolute pools of ADP and AMP rise noticeably. This disproportionate AMP increase serves as a sensitive signal that activates energy-generating pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: During exercise, ATP is buffered and only drops modestly, but the small absolute pools of ADP and AMP rise noticeably. This disproportionate AMP increase serves as a sensitive signal that activates energy-generating pathways.

questions.stem: In the insulin secretion sequence, blood glucose rises first. Which event occurs IMMEDIATELY next?

choices.label: A
choices.text: GLUT2 transporter is activated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Plasma membrane Ca2+ channels open

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP levels rise

choices.label: D
choices.text: K+ leak channels are inhibited

choices.label: E
choices.text: Membrane depolarizes

explanation.summary: After blood glucose rises, glucose enters the beta-cell via GLUT2, is oxidatively catabolized to raise ATP, which inhibits K+ channels, depolarizes the membrane, opens Ca2+ channels, and triggers insulin secretion. GLUT2 uptake is the first step in this ordered cascade.
explanation.whyCorrect: After blood glucose rises, glucose enters the beta-cell via GLUT2, is oxidatively catabolized to raise ATP, which inhibits K+ channels, depolarizes the membrane, opens Ca2+ channels, and triggers insulin secretion. GLUT2 uptake is the first step in this ordered cascade.

questions.stem: This figure shows three sequential forms of the insulin molecule. What byproduct is released during the final maturation step and is clinically useful?

choices.label: B
choices.text: C-peptide, a marker of endogenous insulin production

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chain A, discarded before secretion

choices.label: D
choices.text: The signal sequence, cleaved to form proinsulin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chain B, converted to glucagon

explanation.summary: Preproinsulin loses its signal sequence to become proinsulin; proteolytic cleavage of proinsulin removes the connecting C-peptide, yielding mature insulin (chains A and B joined by disulfide bonds). C-peptide is co-secreted and used to gauge endogenous insulin production.
explanation.whyCorrect: Preproinsulin loses its signal sequence to become proinsulin; proteolytic cleavage of proinsulin removes the connecting C-peptide, yielding mature insulin (chains A and B joined by disulfide bonds). C-peptide is co-secreted and used to gauge endogenous insulin production.

questions.stem: Both branches of this figure share a common cofactor requirement at their initial hydroxylation steps. What cofactor is regenerated as BH2 in phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan hydroxylation?

questions.stem: This pathway produces three catecholamines from tyrosine. Which final product is made predominantly in the adrenal medulla via a SAM-dependent methylation step?

questions.stem: This pathway synthesizes a neurotransmitter from tryptophan via 5-hydroxytryptophan. Besides regulating mood and sleep in the brain, where else is this product predominantly formed and what does it do there?

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: In platelets, where it promotes aggregation and vasoconstriction

In the brain it regulates sleep, mood, and appetite; in platelets it is stored and released to promote aggregation and vasoconstriction, and it also stimulates smooth muscle contraction.

explanation.whyCorrect: Nitric oxide synthase converts arginine to citrulline plus nitric oxide (NO). NO diffuses into vascular smooth muscle and activates guanylate cyclase, raising cGMP and causing relaxation and vasodilation.

questions.stem: This enzyme interconverts pyruvate and lactate while cycling NADH and NAD+. Under anaerobic conditions, what is the main metabolic purpose of the forward (pyruvate to lactate) reaction?

choices.text: Generate ATP directly from lactate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Produce oxygen for the electron transport chain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Synthesize glucose from amino acids

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regenerate NAD+ so glycolysis can continue producing ATP

explanation.summary: Lactate dehydrogenase reduces pyruvate to lactate, oxidizing NADH back to NAD+. Regenerating NAD+ allows glycolysis (which needs NAD+ at the GAPDH step) to keep running and yield ATP when oxygen or mitochondria are unavailable.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lactate dehydrogenase reduces pyruvate to lactate, oxidizing NADH back to NAD+. Regenerating NAD+ allows glycolysis (which needs NAD+ at the GAPDH step) to keep running and yield ATP when oxygen or mitochondria are unavailable.

questions.stem: This figure contrasts albumin-calcium binding in acidemia versus alkalemia. What mechanism explains the change in ionized calcium during acidemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acidemia increases albumin synthesis, buffering ionized calcium levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acidemia stimulates PTH-independent calcium excretion by the kidney

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acidemia increases albumin's affinity for calcium, lowering ionized calcium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excess H+ protonates albumin's binding sites, displacing bound calcium and raising the ionized fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: H+ directly binds free ionized calcium, reducing its concentration

explanation.summary: In acidemia, excess H+ competes with Ca2+ for the negatively charged binding sites on albumin. Protons occupy these sites and displace calcium, so more calcium exists in the free ionized form. In alkalemia the opposite occurs and ionized calcium falls, which can precipitate tetany.
explanation.whyCorrect: In acidemia, excess H+ competes with Ca2+ for the negatively charged binding sites on albumin. Protons occupy these sites and displace calcium, so more calcium exists in the free ionized form. In alkalemia the opposite occurs and ionized calcium falls, which can precipitate tetany.

questions.stem: This diagram depicts hormonal regulation of blood calcium. When blood calcium rises too high, which hormone is released and what is its net effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: PTH, which lowers blood calcium by increasing bone resorption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcitonin, which raises blood calcium by stimulating bone resorption

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calcitriol, which lowers blood calcium by reducing intestinal absorption

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calcitonin, which lowers blood calcium by promoting bone deposition and reducing renal and intestinal calcium uptake

choices.label: E
choices.text: PTH, which raises blood calcium by stimulating calcitonin release

explanation.summary: Calcitonin, secreted by thyroid C cells when calcium is high, drives calcium into bone, reduces renal reabsorption, and reduces intestinal uptake, lowering serum calcium. PTH and calcitriol act in the opposite direction to raise calcium when it is too low.
explanation.whyCorrect: Calcitonin, secreted by thyroid C cells when calcium is high, drives calcium into bone, reduces renal reabsorption, and reduces intestinal uptake, lowering serum calcium. PTH and calcitriol act in the opposite direction to raise calcium when it is too low.

questions.stem: In this scheme of interorgan nitrogen transport, peripheral tissues such as muscle package excess ammonia and export it to the liver primarily in the form of which molecule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glutamine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Free ammonium (NH4+)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspartate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alpha-ketoglutarate

explanation.summary: Glutamine synthetase in muscle and peripheral tissue fixes NH4+ onto glutamate to form glutamine, the main nontoxic carrier of nitrogen in blood. In the liver, mitochondrial glutaminase releases the ammonia again for urea production, while in the kidney it supports acid-base balance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glutamine synthetase in muscle and peripheral tissue fixes NH4+ onto glutamate to form glutamine, the main nontoxic carrier of nitrogen in blood. In the liver, mitochondrial glutaminase releases the ammonia again for urea production, while in the kidney it supports acid-base balance.

questions.stem: This figure summarizes the major sources feeding the body's ammonia pool. Which numbered source depends on bacterial urease acting on urea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gut metabolism (intestinal bacteria)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adenosine deaminase activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deamination of histidine and threonine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glutamate dehydrogenase in the liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Purine and pyrimidine metabolism in muscle

explanation.summary: Intestinal bacteria express urease, which hydrolyzes urea into two molecules of ammonia plus CO2 (source 2). This gut-derived ammonia is a major contributor to the systemic pool, which is why nonabsorbable antibiotics and lactulose are used to lower ammonia in hepatic encephalopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intestinal bacteria express urease, which hydrolyzes urea into two molecules of ammonia plus CO2 (source 2). This gut-derived ammonia is a major contributor to the systemic pool, which is why nonabsorbable antibiotics and lactulose are used to lower ammonia in hepatic encephalopathy.

questions.stem: This figure shows how glutamine synthetase and glutamate dehydrogenase respond to rising ammonia. Why does sustained hyperammonemia impair neurotransmission in the brain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess NAD(P)H shuts down all mitochondrial ATP synthesis in neurons

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glutaminase overactivity floods the synapse with excess glutamate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glutamate is continuously siphoned to make glutamine, depleting glutamate available as a neurotransmitter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ammonia directly binds and blocks postsynaptic glutamate receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ammonia is converted to urea within neurons, consuming acetylcholine

explanation.summary: As ammonia rises, glutamine synthetase consumes glutamate (and ATP) to fix NH3 into glutamine. GDH is pushed toward glutamate formation to replenish it, at the expense of alpha-ketoglutarate, slowing the TCA cycle. The ongoing diversion of glutamate lowers the pool available for neurotransmission.
explanation.whyCorrect: As ammonia rises, glutamine synthetase consumes glutamate (and ATP) to fix NH3 into glutamine. GDH is pushed toward glutamate formation to replenish it, at the expense of alpha-ketoglutarate, slowing the TCA cycle. The ongoing diversion of glutamate lowers the pool available for neurotransmission.

questions.stem: This diagram shows the three major fates of an amino acid. The amino (nitrogen) group removed during degradation is ultimately disposed of through which pathway shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conversion into fatty acids for storage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Synthesis of heme and nitrogenous bases

choices.label: C
choices.text: The urea cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct excretion as free ammonia in bile

choices.label: E
choices.text: Incorporation into carbon skeletons for energy storage

explanation.summary: After transamination/deamination, the liberated nitrogen enters the urea cycle to form urea for excretion. The carbon skeleton is used for energy or storage, while the nitrogen atoms can also be reused to build nitrogen-containing biomolecules such as heme and nucleotide bases.
explanation.whyCorrect: After transamination/deamination, the liberated nitrogen enters the urea cycle to form urea for excretion. The carbon skeleton is used for energy or storage, while the nitrogen atoms can also be reused to build nitrogen-containing biomolecules such as heme and nucleotide bases.

questions.stem: Four key nitrogen-handling enzyme reactions are shown. Which of these reactions requires pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6) as its cofactor?

explanation.summary: Transaminases transfer an amino group between an amino acid and an alpha-keto acid and depend on pyridoxal phosphate (PLP, from vitamin B6). Glutamate dehydrogenase uses NAD(P)+, glutamine synthetase uses ATP, and glutaminase simply hydrolyzes glutamine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transaminases transfer an amino group between an amino acid and an alpha-keto acid and depend on pyridoxal phosphate (PLP, from vitamin B6). Glutamate dehydrogenase uses NAD(P)+, glutamine synthetase uses ATP, and glutaminase simply hydrolyzes glutamine.

questions.stem: This figure shows the liver's response to a hormone during fasting. Which hormone drives the coordinated increase in amino acid degradation, gluconeogenesis, and urea cycle flux depicted here?

explanation.summary: Glucagon, released during fasting, stimulates hepatic amino acid degradation. The carbon skeletons feed gluconeogenesis to release glucose, while the liberated nitrogen (NH4+) is channeled into the urea cycle for disposal. Insulin would promote the opposite, anabolic state.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucagon, released during fasting, stimulates hepatic amino acid degradation. The carbon skeletons feed gluconeogenesis to release glucose, while the liberated nitrogen (NH4+) is channeled into the urea cycle for disposal. Insulin would promote the opposite, anabolic state.

questions.stem: This figure depicts a cycle in which muscle exports nitrogen and a three-carbon unit to the liver, which returns glucose. What is this cycle called?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cori cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucose-alanine (Cahill) cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Urea cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Citric acid (Krebs) cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Malate-aspartate shuttle

explanation.summary: In the glucose-alanine (Cahill) cycle, muscle transaminates pyruvate to alanine using ALT, exporting both carbon and nitrogen to the liver. The liver reconverts alanine to pyruvate for gluconeogenesis and channels the nitrogen into urea. This differs from the Cori cycle, which shuttles lactate rather than alanine and carries no nitrogen.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the glucose-alanine (Cahill) cycle, muscle transaminates pyruvate to alanine using ALT, exporting both carbon and nitrogen to the liver. The liver reconverts alanine to pyruvate for gluconeogenesis and channels the nitrogen into urea. This differs from the Cori cycle, which shuttles lactate rather than alanine and carries no nitrogen.

questions.stem: This diagram overlays two muscle-liver shuttles. Compared with the other cycle shown, what distinguishes the Cahill cycle carrier?

choices.label: E
choices.text: It transports lactate rather than an amino acid

explanation.summary: The Cahill cycle shuttles alanine, carrying the nitrogen (N) group along with a three-carbon skeleton from muscle to the liver, where the nitrogen enters urea. The Cori cycle instead shuttles lactate and moves no nitrogen. Both return glucose to the muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Cahill cycle shuttles alanine, carrying the nitrogen (N) group along with a three-carbon skeleton from muscle to the liver, where the nitrogen enters urea. The Cori cycle instead shuttles lactate and moves no nitrogen. Both return glucose to the muscle.

questions.stem: This schematic shows why excess carbamoyl phosphate from a urea-cycle enzyme defect spills into pyrimidine synthesis, bypassing the negative-feedback point. questions.stem: This overview describes the free amino acid pool as 'dynamic but stable.' Which statement best explains that description?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It represents a one-way flow of dietary protein into tissue protein

choices.label: B
choices.text: It contains only essential amino acids maintained purely by dietary intake

choices.label: C
choices.text: Its composition is fixed because amino acids cannot be interconverted

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amino acids are held in a dedicated storage organelle and released only during starvation

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is continuously replenished by protein turnover and diet while being drained by biosynthesis and catabolism, keeping its size roughly constant

explanation.summary: The pool is in constant flux: inputs (protein turnover and dietary intake) are balanced by outputs (protein synthesis, nonprotein nitrogen compounds, and catabolism to fuels and urea), so its concentration stays stable despite ongoing exchange.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pool is in constant flux: inputs (protein turnover and dietary intake) are balanced by outputs (protein synthesis, nonprotein nitrogen compounds, and catabolism to fuels and urea), so its concentration stays stable despite ongoing exchange.

questions.stem: This diagram localizes the urea cycle to hepatocyte mitochondria and cytosol. Deficiency of the enzyme that combines ornithine with carbamoyl phosphate to form citrulline (OTC) is inherited in which pattern?
