choices.text: Most body iron is stored in the liver as ferritin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dietary iron is absorbed with nearly 100% efficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron is freely excreted by the kidney when in excess

explanation.summary: Because iron losses are small and unregulated (~1 mg/day), total body iron is controlled almost entirely at the level of intestinal absorption. Most iron is in circulating hemoglobin, and macrophages recycle iron from senescent red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because iron losses are small and unregulated (~1 mg/day), total body iron is controlled almost entirely at the level of intestinal absorption. Most iron is in circulating hemoglobin, and macrophages recycle iron from senescent red cells.

questions.stem: In this model of intestinal iron handling, the liver-derived hormone acting on ferroportin 1 (red inhibitory bar) is a master regulator of iron. What is the net effect of increasing this hormone?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased iron absorption into the bloodstream

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased release of stored iron from macrophages

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased plasma transferrin synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased iron export into the blood, lowering serum iron

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced reduction of dietary Fe3+ to Fe2+

explanation.summary: Hepcidin binds and degrades ferroportin, blocking iron export from enterocytes and macrophages. High hepcidin (e.g., in inflammation) traps iron in cells and lowers serum iron, contributing to anemia of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepcidin binds and degrades ferroportin, blocking iron export from enterocytes and macrophages. High hepcidin (e.g., in inflammation) traps iron in cells and lowers serum iron, contributing to anemia of chronic disease.

questions.stem: This diagram illustrates how ferritin translation is controlled by iron. By what mechanism does a rise in intracellular iron increase ferritin production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Iron binds the IRE directly, recruiting ribosomes to the mRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Iron binds the iron-response element binding protein (IRE-BP), causing it to release from the IRE so the mRNA can be translated

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iron stabilizes ferritin mRNA against degradation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iron phosphorylates ferritin protein to activate it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron increases transcription of the ferritin gene in the nucleus

explanation.summary: When iron is low, IRE-BP occupies the 5' IRE and blocks translation. When iron is abundant, it binds IRE-BP, which releases the IRE, permitting ribosome loading and ferritin synthesis so excess iron can be safely stored.
explanation.whyCorrect: When iron is low, IRE-BP occupies the 5' IRE and blocks translation. When iron is abundant, it binds IRE-BP, which releases the IRE, permitting ribosome loading and ferritin synthesis so excess iron can be safely stored.

questions.stem: This diagram shows that in the low-iron state, IRP binds the IREs on transferrin receptor (TfR) mRNA, stabilizing it and increasing TfR production. Under this same low-iron condition, what happens to ferritin synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ferritin synthesis decreases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ferritin synthesis increases in parallel with TfR

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ferritin is exported from the cell instead of stored

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ferritin synthesis is unchanged

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ferritin mRNA is degraded but protein rises

explanation.summary: The same iron-regulatory proteins reciprocally control these mRNAs. In iron deficiency, IRP binding stabilizes TfR mRNA (more receptors to import iron) but blocks translation of ferritin mRNA (less storage protein), coordinating uptake and storage inversely.
explanation.whyCorrect: The same iron-regulatory proteins reciprocally control these mRNAs. In iron deficiency, IRP binding stabilizes TfR mRNA (more receptors to import iron) but blocks translation of ferritin mRNA (less storage protein), coordinating uptake and storage inversely.

questions.stem: This diagram shows an enzyme acting on a glycogen molecule during its breakdown. What is the specific action of the debranching enzyme depicted here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It cleaves alpha-1,4 bonds to release glucose 1-phosphate

choices.label: B
choices.text: It creates alpha-1,6 branch points from a linear chain

choices.label: C
choices.text: It adds UDP-glucose to create new alpha-1,4 linkages

choices.label: D
choices.text: It phosphorylates terminal glucose residues at branch points

choices.label: E
choices.text: It transfers a block of glucose units to another chain and hydrolyzes the remaining alpha-1,6 linkage

explanation.summary: Glycogen phosphorylase cannot cleave near branch points, so the bifunctional debranching enzyme has 4-alpha-glucanotransferase activity (moves a set of glucose units to a neighboring chain) and alpha-1,6-glucosidase activity (hydrolyzes the exposed branch linkage), freeing the residues for continued phosphorylase action.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycogen phosphorylase cannot cleave near branch points, so the bifunctional debranching enzyme has 4-alpha-glucanotransferase activity (moves a set of glucose units to a neighboring chain) and alpha-1,6-glucosidase activity (hydrolyzes the exposed branch linkage), freeing the residues for continued phosphorylase action.

questions.stem: The figure shows glycogen phosphorylase releasing glucose 1-phosphate but stalling to leave a shortened, branched core. What is the metabolic significance of this stalled residual structure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It represents lysosomal glycogen resistant to all cytosolic enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the substrate for branching enzyme to add new chains

choices.label: C
choices.text: It marks glycogen that will be exported intact into blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphorylase halts about four residues from a branch, requiring debranching enzyme to continue degradation

choices.label: E
choices.text: It signals that glycogen synthesis has been fully completed

explanation.summary: Glycogen phosphorylase can only cleave alpha-1,4 bonds until roughly four glucose units remain before an alpha-1,6 branch, producing the truncated limit dextrin. Further breakdown then depends on the debranching enzyme, illustrating why its deficiency (Cori disease) causes abnormal glycogen with short outer branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycogen phosphorylase can only cleave alpha-1,4 bonds until roughly four glucose units remain before an alpha-1,6 branch, producing the truncated limit dextrin. Further breakdown then depends on the debranching enzyme, illustrating why its deficiency (Cori disease) causes abnormal glycogen with short outer branches.

questions.stem: This erythrocyte pathway diagram illustrates the defect underlying G6PD deficiency and the resulting oxidative injury. Aside from the Heinz bodies noted here, what characteristic red-cell morphology appears on the peripheral smear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sickle cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schistocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Target cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bite cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spherocytes

explanation.summary: Loss of G6PD reduces NADPH, so glutathione cannot be regenerated to detoxify peroxides, and hemoglobin oxidizes into Heinz bodies. Splenic macrophages pluck these inclusions from the membrane, leaving bite cells. Hemolysis is episodic and triggered by oxidant drugs, infections, or fava beans.
explanation.whyCorrect: Loss of G6PD reduces NADPH, so glutathione cannot be regenerated to detoxify peroxides, and hemoglobin oxidizes into Heinz bodies. Splenic macrophages pluck these inclusions from the membrane, leaving bite cells. Hemolysis is episodic and triggered by oxidant drugs, infections, or fava beans.

questions.stem: This figure depicts a glycolytic enzyme defect that lowers red-cell ATP and produces spiculated, fragile cells. What is the inheritance pattern of this deficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autosomal recessive

choices.label: B
choices.text: X-linked dominant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mitochondrial (maternal)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autosomal dominant

choices.label: E
choices.text: X-linked recessive

explanation.summary: Pyruvate kinase deficiency is autosomal recessive. Because mature RBCs lack mitochondria, they depend on glycolysis for ATP; without pyruvate kinase, ATP falls, membrane ion pumps fail, and cells become rigid, echinocytic, and prone to extravascular hemolysis. (Contrast with X-linked recessive G6PD deficiency.)
explanation.whyCorrect: Pyruvate kinase deficiency is autosomal recessive. Because mature RBCs lack mitochondria, they depend on glycolysis for ATP; without pyruvate kinase, ATP falls, membrane ion pumps fail, and cells become rigid, echinocytic, and prone to extravascular hemolysis. (Contrast with X-linked recessive G6PD deficiency.)

questions.stem: This red-cell metabolic map highlights a side branch off 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate (the Rapoport-Luebering shunt). What is the functional purpose of the product generated by this shunt?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reduces methemoglobin back to functional hemoglobin

choices.label: B
choices.text: It generates ATP to power the Na/K-ATPase directly

choices.label: C
choices.text: It generates NADPH to protect against oxidative stress

choices.label: D
choices.text: It produces lactate to regenerate NAD+ for glycolysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: It generates 2,3-BPG, which lowers hemoglobin's oxygen affinity to promote tissue oxygen unloading

explanation.summary: The Rapoport-Luebering shunt diverts 1,3-BPG to 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate via bisphosphoglycerate mutase, at the cost of net ATP from that step. 2,3-BPG binds deoxyhemoglobin and stabilizes the T state, shifting the oxygen dissociation curve rightward to enhance oxygen delivery to tissues, especially at altitude or in chronic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Rapoport-Luebering shunt diverts 1,3-BPG to 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate via bisphosphoglycerate mutase, at the cost of net ATP from that step. 2,3-BPG binds deoxyhemoglobin and stabilizes the T state, shifting the oxygen dissociation curve rightward to enhance oxygen delivery to tissues, especially at altitude or in chronic anemia.

questions.stem: This numbered map of glycogen metabolism marks the enzyme (1) that converts glucose 6-phosphate to free glucose for export via GLUT2. Deficiency of this enzyme causes which classic disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cori disease (type III)

choices.label: B
choices.text: McArdle disease (type V)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Andersen disease (type IV)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pompe disease (type II)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Von Gierke disease (type I glycogen storage disease)

explanation.summary: Enzyme (1) is glucose-6-phosphatase, the only step allowing the liver to release free glucose from both glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Its deficiency (von Gierke, type I) causes severe fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and hyperlipidemia, since glucose 6-phosphate cannot be dephosphorylated for export.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enzyme (1) is glucose-6-phosphatase, the only step allowing the liver to release free glucose from both glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Its deficiency (von Gierke, type I) causes severe fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and hyperlipidemia, since glucose 6-phosphate cannot be dephosphorylated for export.

questions.stem: This lipoprotein transport map shows where PCSK9 acts on the hepatic LDL receptor. What is the consequence of PCSK9 binding the LDL receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It enhances ApoE receptor uptake of chylomicron remnants

choices.label: B
choices.text: It stimulates HMG-CoA reductase to increase cholesterol synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It recycles the LDL receptor to the surface, lowering plasma LDL

choices.label: D
choices.text: It activates lipoprotein lipase to hydrolyze VLDL triglycerides

choices.label: E
choices.text: It promotes lysosomal degradation of the LDL receptor, reducing LDL clearance and raising plasma LDL

explanation.summary: PCSK9 binds the hepatic LDL receptor and targets it for lysosomal destruction rather than recycling, so fewer receptors return to the surface and LDL clearance falls, raising plasma LDL. PCSK9 inhibitors block this degradation, increasing receptor availability and lowering LDL, explaining the diagram's role for familial hypercholesterolemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: PCSK9 binds the hepatic LDL receptor and targets it for lysosomal destruction rather than recycling, so fewer receptors return to the surface and LDL clearance falls, raising plasma LDL. PCSK9 inhibitors block this degradation, increasing receptor availability and lowering LDL, explaining the diagram's role for familial hypercholesterolemia.

questions.stem: What is the primary metabolic function carried out by the organelle diagrammed here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ribosome assembly and rRNA synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lysosomal degradation of macromolecules

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP synthesis via oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detoxification and lipid synthesis on smooth ER

choices.label: E
choices.text: Protein glycosylation and packaging for secretion

explanation.summary: The mitochondrion carries out oxidative phosphorylation along the electron transport chain located on the cristae of the inner membrane, generating the bulk of cellular ATP; the matrix also houses the TCA cycle and fatty acid beta-oxidation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mitochondrion carries out oxidative phosphorylation along the electron transport chain located on the cristae of the inner membrane, generating the bulk of cellular ATP; the matrix also houses the TCA cycle and fatty acid beta-oxidation.

questions.stem: This karyotype shows an extra copy of chromosome 21 (circled). What meiotic error most commonly gives rise to this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Balanced reciprocal translocation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Single-gene point mutation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chromosomal deletion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Meiotic nondisjunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ring chromosome formation

explanation.summary: Most cases of trisomy 21 result from meiotic nondisjunction, the failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate, producing a gamete with an extra chromosome 21. Risk rises with advanced maternal age.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most cases of trisomy 21 result from meiotic nondisjunction, the failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate, producing a gamete with an extra chromosome 21. Risk rises with advanced maternal age.

questions.stem: This diagram shows chromosomes 4 and 20 exchanging segments. This type of rearrangement between two non-homologous chromosomes is specifically called a:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Terminal deletion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pericentric inversion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reciprocal translocation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tandem duplication

choices.label: E
choices.text: Robertsonian fusion

explanation.summary: A reciprocal translocation is a mutual exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes. When no genetic material is net gained or lost the carrier is usually phenotypically normal, but their gametes may be unbalanced.
explanation.whyCorrect: A reciprocal translocation is a mutual exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes. When no genetic material is net gained or lost the carrier is usually phenotypically normal, but their gametes may be unbalanced.

questions.stem: A person carries a balanced reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 12 and 14 with no net loss of genetic material. What is the main clinical risk this poses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Progressive neurodegeneration in the carrier

choices.label: B
choices.text: Producing gametes with unbalanced chromosome content, risking miscarriage or an affected child

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complete infertility in all carriers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous development of trisomy 21 in the carrier

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediate development of leukemia in the carrier

explanation.summary: Balanced translocation carriers are typically phenotypically normal because total genetic material is preserved. During meiosis, however, segregation can yield gametes with duplicated or deleted segments (unbalanced), increasing the risk of recurrent pregnancy loss or a child with a chromosomal disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Balanced translocation carriers are typically phenotypically normal because total genetic material is preserved. During meiosis, however, segregation can yield gametes with duplicated or deleted segments (unbalanced), increasing the risk of recurrent pregnancy loss or a child with a chromosomal disorder.

questions.stem: The structural chromosome abnormality shown on the right-hand chromosome results in which change to the cell's genetic dosage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain of genetic material (partial trisomy of the affected segment)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fusion of two non-homologous chromosomes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exchange of material between homologous chromosomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reversal of gene order with no dosage change

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of genetic material for the affected segment

explanation.summary: A duplication adds an extra copy of a chromosomal segment, increasing gene dosage for that region and producing a partial trisomy, unlike deletions (loss) or inversions (no net dosage change).
explanation.whyCorrect: A duplication adds an extra copy of a chromosomal segment, increasing gene dosage for that region and producing a partial trisomy, unlike deletions (loss) or inversions (no net dosage change).

questions.stem: In the process shown, a chromosomal segment that does NOT include the centromere is excised, flipped, and reinserted. Which structural abnormality does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reciprocal translocation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tandem duplication

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paracentric inversion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Terminal deletion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pericentric inversion

explanation.summary: An inversion in which the reversed segment lies entirely on one arm and excludes the centromere is a paracentric inversion; a pericentric inversion would span the centromere.
explanation.whyCorrect: An inversion in which the reversed segment lies entirely on one arm and excludes the centromere is a paracentric inversion; a pericentric inversion would span the centromere.

questions.stem: In the sequences shown, a gamete labeled 'n+1' (carrying an extra chromosome) is fertilized by a normal gamete. What is the chromosomal constitution of the resulting zygote?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal diploid (2n)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tetrasomy (2n+2)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trisomy (2n+1)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Triploidy (3n)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Monosomy (2n-1)

explanation.summary: An n+1 gamete already carries one extra chromosome; fertilization by a normal (n) gamete yields a 2n+1 zygote, i.e., trisomy for that chromosome.
explanation.whyCorrect: An n+1 gamete already carries one extra chromosome; fertilization by a normal (n) gamete yields a 2n+1 zygote, i.e., trisomy for that chromosome.

questions.stem: The faulty chromosome separation illustrated here occurs after fertilization during ordinary somatic cell division. The clinical severity of the resulting condition depends chiefly on which factor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sex of the affected individual

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the error was in meiosis I or meiosis II

choices.label: C
choices.text: The proportion and tissue distribution of the abnormal cell line

choices.label: D
choices.text: Which parent contributed the abnormal chromosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: The presence of crossing over

explanation.summary: Post-zygotic (mitotic) nondisjunction creates two or more genetically distinct cell lines (mosaicism); phenotype severity varies with how many cells and which tissues carry the abnormal line.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-zygotic (mitotic) nondisjunction creates two or more genetically distinct cell lines (mosaicism); phenotype severity varies with how many cells and which tissues carry the abnormal line.

questions.stem: When the two non-homologous acrocentric chromosomes shown fuse to form the derivative chromosome, which portions are typically lost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The telomeres of the long arms

choices.label: B
choices.text: One entire chromosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: The short (p) arms of both chromosomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: The long (q) arms of both chromosomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both centromeres

explanation.summary: In a Robertsonian translocation the long arms of two acrocentric chromosomes fuse near the centromere and the small short arms (carrying redundant rRNA genes) are lost, producing a balanced carrier with 45 chromosomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a Robertsonian translocation the long arms of two acrocentric chromosomes fuse near the centromere and the small short arms (carrying redundant rRNA genes) are lost, producing a balanced carrier with 45 chromosomes.

questions.stem: In this ideogram, colored bands link specific regions of the human X and Y chromosomes to one another. What do these linked, shared segments most directly permit during male meiosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitotic nondisjunction of the sex chromosomes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pairing and recombination between X and Y in the pseudoautosomal regions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complete synapsis along the entire length of X and Y

choices.label: D
choices.text: Silencing of the entire Y chromosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fusion of X and Y into a single metacentric chromosome

explanation.summary: The X and Y chromosomes share small homologous pseudoautosomal regions. These segments synapse and undergo obligate crossing over during meiosis, ensuring proper X-Y segregation; failure here contributes to sex-chromosome aneuploidy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The X and Y chromosomes share small homologous pseudoautosomal regions. These segments synapse and undergo obligate crossing over during meiosis, ensuring proper X-Y segregation; failure here contributes to sex-chromosome aneuploidy.

questions.stem: Compared with a nondisjunction event in meiosis I (middle), what is distinctive about the gametes produced when nondisjunction occurs in meiosis II (right)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No aneuploid gametes are produced

choices.label: B
choices.text: The abnormal gametes carry two different homologs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only two of the four gametes are abnormal, and the two aneuploid gametes carry identical (sister) chromosomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every gamete receives an extra chromosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: All four gametes are abnormal, as in meiosis I errors

explanation.summary: In meiosis I nondisjunction homologs fail to separate, so all four gametes are abnormal and the extra-chromosome gametes carry both homologs. In meiosis II nondisjunction, sister chromatids fail to separate in one cell only, so two gametes are normal and the two abnormal ones carry duplicate sister chromosomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: In meiosis I nondisjunction homologs fail to separate, so all four gametes are abnormal and the extra-chromosome gametes carry both homologs. In meiosis II nondisjunction, sister chromatids fail to separate in one cell only, so two gametes are normal and the two abnormal ones carry duplicate sister chromosomes.

questions.stem: Using the sex-chromosome combinations shown, how many Barr bodies would be expected in the somatic cells of the individual with a 47,XXY karyotype?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depends on the parental origin of the Y

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three

choices.label: D
choices.text: None

choices.label: E
choices.text: One

explanation.summary: The number of Barr bodies equals the number of X chromosomes minus one. A 47,XXY (Klinefelter) individual has two X chromosomes, so one X is inactivated, producing a single Barr body.
explanation.whyCorrect: The number of Barr bodies equals the number of X chromosomes minus one. A 47,XXY (Klinefelter) individual has two X chromosomes, so one X is inactivated, producing a single Barr body.

questions.stem: The events depicted here (moving a segment to a new location, losing a segment, gaining an extra copy, flipping a segment, and exchanging segments between non-homologous chromosomes) all belong to which single category of mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Single-nucleotide point mutations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trinucleotide repeat expansions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epigenetic silencing events

choices.label: D
choices.text: Numerical aneuploidies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Structural chromosomal aberrations

explanation.summary: Insertion, deletion, duplication, inversion, and translocation all alter chromosome structure rather than chromosome number or a single base, so they are collectively classified as structural chromosomal aberrations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Insertion, deletion, duplication, inversion, and translocation all alter chromosome structure rather than chromosome number or a single base, so they are collectively classified as structural chromosomal aberrations.

questions.stem: In this family, both parents are unaffected but their daughter shows a patchy, mosaic distribution of an abnormality. What does this pattern most strongly suggest about the origin of her mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A de novo post-zygotic mutation producing two genetically distinct cell lines

choices.label: B
choices.text: An autosomal recessive mutation inherited from both carrier parents

choices.label: C
choices.text: A germline mutation present in all of her cells equally

choices.label: D
choices.text: A mitochondrial mutation inherited from the mother

choices.label: E
choices.text: An X-linked dominant mutation inherited from the father

explanation.summary: A patchy (mosaic) phenotype in a child of two unaffected parents indicates a mutation arising after fertilization (post-zygotic), so only a subset of cells carry it. Because the parents lack the mutation, recurrence risk in siblings is low unless a parent has gonadal mosaicism.
explanation.whyCorrect: A patchy (mosaic) phenotype in a child of two unaffected parents indicates a mutation arising after fertilization (post-zygotic), so only a subset of cells carry it. Because the parents lack the mutation, recurrence risk in siblings is low unless a parent has gonadal mosaicism.

questions.stem: This figure contrasts two forms of mosaicism. Why does the form on the left (mutation in a fraction of germ cells) carry a very different reproductive implication than the form on the right (mutation limited to somatic tissue)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only somatic mosaicism can be detected on prenatal testing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Germline mosaicism can be transmitted to offspring, whereas a purely somatic mutation cannot be passed on

choices.label: C
choices.text: Somatic mosaicism is always more severe than germline mosaicism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Germline mosaicism only affects one organ, whereas somatic mosaicism is generalized

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both are equally heritable to all offspring

explanation.summary: In germline (gonadal) mosaicism a fraction of gametes carry the mutation, so unaffected-appearing parents can have affected children and may have recurrence. A somatic mutation is confined to body tissues and cannot enter the germline, so it is not transmitted.
explanation.whyCorrect: In germline (gonadal) mosaicism a fraction of gametes carry the mutation, so unaffected-appearing parents can have affected children and may have recurrence. A somatic mutation is confined to body tissues and cannot enter the germline, so it is not transmitted.

questions.stem: In panel B, an abnormal X chromosome lacking its inactivation center fails to undergo silencing, resulting in biallelic expression. What normally coats the future inactive X to trigger the spreading silencing seen in panel A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: XIST non-coding RNA transcribed from the X-inactivation center

choices.label: B
choices.text: Telomerase extending the chromosome ends

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA polymerase recruited to the centromere

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spliceosomal small nuclear RNA

choices.label: E
choices.text: A histone acetyltransferase that opens chromatin

explanation.summary: The X-inactivation center encodes XIST, a long non-coding RNA that coats the chromosome in cis and recruits repressive chromatin modifications, spreading epigenetic silencing across most genes. Without an intact XIC/XIST, the X cannot be inactivated and both alleles are expressed.
explanation.whyCorrect: The X-inactivation center encodes XIST, a long non-coding RNA that coats the chromosome in cis and recruits repressive chromatin modifications, spreading epigenetic silencing across most genes. Without an intact XIC/XIST, the X cannot be inactivated and both alleles are expressed.

questions.stem: This figure shows that in each early female embryonic cell either the paternal or the maternal X is randomly inactivated and then maintained through all daughter cells. What is the most important consequence of this process for X-linked gene expression in females?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The inactive X is permanently deleted from the genome

choices.label: B
choices.text: X inactivation reverses at each cell division

choices.label: C
choices.text: All cells in a female express only the paternal X

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every cell in a female expresses both X chromosomes equally

choices.label: E
choices.text: Females are functional mosaics, with patches of cells expressing either the maternal or paternal X

explanation.summary: Because the choice of which X to inactivate is random per cell and is clonally maintained, a female is a mosaic of two cell populations. This underlies variable expression of X-linked disorders in heterozygous females and phenomena such as patchy skin findings.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because the choice of which X to inactivate is random per cell and is clonally maintained, a female is a mosaic of two cell populations. This underlies variable expression of X-linked disorders in heterozygous females and phenomena such as patchy skin findings.

questions.stem: The histogram plots the proportion of females against their X-inactivation ratio. What does the bell-shaped distribution centered near 50:50 indicate about X inactivation in the female population?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most females show extreme skewing toward one X

choices.label: B
choices.text: X inactivation ratios are fixed at exactly 50:50 in every female

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ratio is determined solely by the number of Barr bodies

choices.label: D
choices.text: X inactivation is normally random, so most females have a roughly balanced ratio and markedly skewed inactivation is uncommon

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nearly all females preferentially inactivate the paternal X

explanation.summary: Because the initial choice of which X to inactivate is random, most females cluster around a balanced 50:50 ratio, with roughly 90% falling between 75:25 and 25:75. Strongly skewed inactivation is uncommon and can unmask X-linked disease in carrier females.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because the initial choice of which X to inactivate is random, most females cluster around a balanced 50:50 ratio, with roughly 90% falling between 75:25 and 25:75. Strongly skewed inactivation is uncommon and can unmask X-linked disease in carrier females.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a cell carrying one normal X and one structurally abnormal X, with the abnormal X preferentially becoming the condensed (inactivated) chromosome in every surviving lineage. What is the most likely functional consequence of this skewed selection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The autosomes compensate by doubling their expression

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cell becomes functionally monosomic for all X-linked genes

choices.label: C
choices.text: The active X in most cells is the normal one, mitigating the phenotype of the abnormal X

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both X chromosomes remain transcriptionally active in these cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: The abnormal X is physically deleted from the genome

explanation.summary: Cells that inactivate the abnormal (e.g., partially deleted or unbalanced) X have a selective advantage, so the normal X stays active. This nonrandom (skewed) X-inactivation blunts the phenotypic impact of the structural abnormality.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cells that inactivate the abnormal (e.g., partially deleted or unbalanced) X have a selective advantage, so the normal X stays active. This nonrandom (skewed) X-inactivation blunts the phenotypic impact of the structural abnormality.

questions.stem: The lineage tree shows a single mutational event in one dividing cell whose descendants all carry the change while the rest of the population remains normal. What phenomenon does this illustrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A clonal population of mutant cells creating somatic mosaicism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reversion of the mutation in later generations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Meiotic nondisjunction producing aneuploidy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Uniform mutation of every cell in the tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Germline transmission of the mutation to all offspring

explanation.summary: A post-zygotic mutation is inherited only by the daughter cells of the mutated cell, generating a clone of mutant cells alongside normal cells. The result is a somatic mosaic individual with two genetically distinct populations.
explanation.whyCorrect: A post-zygotic mutation is inherited only by the daughter cells of the mutated cell, generating a clone of mutant cells alongside normal cells. The result is a somatic mosaic individual with two genetically distinct populations.

questions.stem: The figure contrasts three distributions of a post-zygotic mutation within one person's tissues. What ultimately determines whether such a mosaic mutation can be transmitted to that person's children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the mutation is present in the gamete-forming (germline) cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the individual shows any clinical phenotype

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the mutation lies on an autosome versus a sex chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the mutation affects a large fraction of skin cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the mutation arose before or after birth

explanation.summary: Transmission to offspring requires the mutation to be present in the germline. A purely somatic mosaic cannot pass it on, whereas germline (gonadal) involvement allows an unaffected or affected parent to have affected children.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transmission to offspring requires the mutation to be present in the germline. A purely somatic mosaic cannot pass it on, whereas germline (gonadal) involvement allows an unaffected or affected parent to have affected children.

questions.stem: In this pedigree the trait appears in every generation, affects males and females roughly equally, and each affected person has an affected parent. What is the most likely mode of inheritance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autosomal recessive

choices.label: B
choices.text: X-linked recessive

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mitochondrial

choices.label: D
choices.text: X-linked dominant

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autosomal dominant

explanation.summary: Vertical transmission through every generation with both sexes affected and each affected individual having an affected parent is the hallmark of autosomal dominant inheritance. Male-to-male transmission would further exclude X-linkage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vertical transmission through every generation with both sexes affected and each affected individual having an affected parent is the hallmark of autosomal dominant inheritance. Male-to-male transmission would further exclude X-linkage.

questions.stem: This pedigree marks many unaffected individuals as carriers (central dot), with affected individuals (filled) appearing in a single sibship born to phenotypically normal parents. What inheritance pattern is shown?

choices.label: A

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autosomal dominant

choices.label: D
choices.text: Y-linked

choices.label: E
choices.text: X-linked dominant

explanation.summary: Two unaffected heterozygous carriers producing affected children, with the trait skipping generations and affecting both sexes, is characteristic of autosomal recessive inheritance. Carrier status (dot) is emphasized because heterozygotes are clinically silent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Two unaffected heterozygous carriers producing affected children, with the trait skipping generations and affecting both sexes, is characteristic of autosomal recessive inheritance. Carrier status (dot) is emphasized because heterozygotes are clinically silent.

questions.stem: In this pedigree an affected father transmits the trait to all of his daughters but to none of his sons, and there is no male-to-male transmission. What is the most likely mode of inheritance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: X-linked dominant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autosomal dominant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autosomal recessive

choices.label: D
choices.text: Y-linked

choices.label: E
choices.text: X-linked recessive

explanation.summary: An affected male passes his single X to every daughter and his Y to every son, so in X-linked dominant inheritance all daughters and no sons of an affected father are affected. Absence of male-to-male transmission is the key clue.

questions.stem: An affected male's daughters are all unaffected carriers (dot), and affected males reappear in later generations through these carrier daughters, with no father-to-son transmission. What inheritance pattern does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitochondrial

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autosomal dominant

choices.label: C
choices.text: X-linked dominant

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autosomal recessive

choices.label: E

explanation.summary: In X-linked recessive inheritance an affected male makes all daughters obligate carriers; those carrier daughters then transmit the allele to affected sons. The disorder predominantly affects males and skips through unaffected carrier females, with no male-to-male transmission.

questions.stem: The top cell in this figure contains a mixture of mutant (red) and normal (blue) mitochondria, which then randomly segregate to daughter cells. What term describes this coexistence of two mitochondrial genotypes within a single cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anticipation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Homoplasmy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heteroplasmy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Genomic imprinting

choices.label: E
choices.text: Locus heterogeneity

explanation.summary: Heteroplasmy is the presence of both mutant and wild-type mtDNA in one cell. Random segregation during division changes the mutant load; disease appears only once the mutant fraction exceeds a tissue-specific threshold for phenotypic expression, as shown by the disease vs normal phenotype branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heteroplasmy is the presence of both mutant and wild-type mtDNA in one cell. Random segregation during division changes the mutant load; disease appears only once the mutant fraction exceeds a tissue-specific threshold for phenotypic expression, as shown by the disease vs normal phenotype branches.

questions.stem: In this diagram of the electron transport chain, 2,4-dinitrophenol and aspirin overdose are shown acting on the inner mitochondrial membrane. By what mechanism do these agents disrupt ATP production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They block cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV)

choices.label: B
choices.text: They uncouple oxidation from phosphorylation by dissipating the proton gradient

choices.label: C
choices.text: They deplete the pool of NADH and FADH2

choices.label: D
choices.text: They inhibit Complex I electron transfer

choices.label: E
choices.text: They inhibit ATP synthase (Complex V) directly

explanation.summary: Uncouplers act as proton ionophores, letting H+ leak back into the matrix without passing through ATP synthase. Electron transport and oxygen consumption continue (even accelerate), but the energy is released as heat rather than captured as ATP, causing hyperthermia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uncouplers act as proton ionophores, letting H+ leak back into the matrix without passing through ATP synthase. Electron transport and oxygen consumption continue (even accelerate), but the energy is released as heat rather than captured as ATP, causing hyperthermia.
